tmpls.name: Card 1

fields: How can we execute a JAR file?
$ java -jar [file.jar]

A Java Archive, meaning a package file format typically used to aggregate many Java class files and associated metadata and resources (text, images, etc.) into one file to distribute application software or libraries on the Java platform.

Overclocking is a configuration of computer hardware components to operate faster than certified by the original manufacturer, with "faster" specified as clock frequency. It can be obtained (automatically when needed( by Intel Turbo Boost or AMD Turbo Core technologies.

How can we check if Intel Turbo Boost is enabled?

# This is a double negative; when "no turbo" is off (=0) then Turbo Boost is on
$ cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/intel_pstate/no_turbo 0

$ sudo apt-get install linux-tools-common linux-tools-generic
$ sudo modprobe msr
$ sudo turbostat

# in another terminal, run a loop like this one
$ while :; do :; done.

# check real time CPU Mhz
$ lscpu
$ cat /proc/cpuinfo | grep -i mhz
- The output of cat /proc/cpuinfo does not always show the real current CPU frequency but instead the maximum non-turbo frequency even when Turbo Boost is enabled and active.
What is RAID 1?

RAID 1 (mirroring) consists of an exact copy of a set of data on two or more disks. Common uses: Installation of the operating system or important subdirectories, such as /home.

- Minimum drives: 2

- Fault Tolerance: 1

- Capacity: size_of_smallest_disk

- Performance: increased read performance, but worst write performance (dictated by the slower drive)

- Common cases: small servers, part of a larger cluster
What is user-mode linux?
A linux kernel that can be executed as a user process on a standard linux box (thus, a virtual machine).

For non-persistent (run-time) network configuration, ifconfig (package net-tools), working only on Layer 3, has been superseded by ip (package iproute), working on both Layer 2 and Layer 3. So let's use ip to:

# show information (including statistics) for all network interfaces or just one
# to list all interfaces (/sys/class/net), we could als: $ ip -s link
# statistics could be found in /sys/class/net/eth0/statistics
# -o is for single line output!
$ ip -s -o a show [eth0]
$ ip link show [eth0]

# set the IP address for eth0, the MTU (1500 is ethernet default) and bring it up
# equivalent to $ ifconfig eth0 up 192.168.1.7 netmask 255.255.0.0
$ ip addr add 192.168.1.7/16 [eth0]
$ ip link set [eth0] mtu 1500
$ ip link set [eth0] [up | down]

# show and then set the networking route (alias host routing table)
# the default route is used when no other route matches
# equivalent to Win "route print", while on Mac we would only have "netstat -r"
$ ip route
$ ip route show default

# add a route
# "via" (the gateway) can be omitted
# in this case we are adding a network, but we could add a single host (/32)
$ ip route add 172.16.1.0/24 via 192.168.1.5
"dev" is not always necessary, for but mnemonics consistency, let's just assume it is.
How can we define a permanent (alias boot-time) network configuration (static IP) for an interface? This is a Debian/Ubuntu way.

# etc/network/interfaces in Debian/Ubuntu

auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

auto enp0s3
iface enp0s3 inet static
   address 10.0.2.15
   netmask 255.255.255.0
   gateway 10.0.2.2

# place dns information after interface stanzas
dns-nameservers 8.8.8.8

# syntax check
$ ifup --no-act enp0s3
$ systemctl restart networking

# make changes effective
$ sudo ifdown enp0s3 && sudo ifup enp0s3

Here we have created an intermittent network issue (= packet drops) thanks to the traffic control (tc) command, to be later claned up. Then, we need to prove it's occuring, by sniffing packages to an inteface.

- We need to sniff packages on an interface bound to the ICMP protocol and save them in a file

- Visualize that file (in a graphic environment, we could use Wireshark)

# qdisc is short for 'queueing discipline': whenever the kernel needs to send a packet to an interface, it is enqueued to the qdisc configured for that interface
# in this case, the qdisc is Network Emulator (netem)

# for CentOS6 use 'corrupt 40%' instead of 'random 40'
$ tc qdisc add dev lo root netem loss random 40

# clean
$ tc qdisc del dev lo root

# sniff & write it (-i any would sniff on any device)
$ ping localhost
$ tcpdump -i lo -w capture.pcap proto ICMP

# read it
$ tcpdump -r capture.pcap

pcap consists of an API for capturing network traffic, implemented in Unix-like systems as the libpcap library. The typical file extension for captured traffic is .pcap, although .cap and .dmp are also in common use.

This is how we can create a password-protected ZIP file archive, add something to it, visualize its content and unzip it to a certain target.

What about RAR archives?

# archive
$ zip -r9 archive.zip [file1 | dir1] [file2 | dir1] 

# list (we could also use $ lesspipe archive.zip)
$ unzip -l archive.zip

# unarchive
$ unzip archive.zip [-d dir]

# password protected file saying "unsupported compression method 99" when extracting?
$ 7z x archive.zip

$ rar u archive.rar [file]
$ unrar l archive.rar
$ unrar archive.rar
We have a live USB that won't load, saying "no configuration file" or "No DEFAULT or UI configuration directive found!". What can we try?

Open the USB installation media, then rename the following:

isolinux –> syslinux (folder)
isolinux.bin –> syslinux.bin
isolinux.cfg –> syslinux.cfg
Let's describe the boot process, according to the old MBR model.

Power-On Self Test: when the computer is powered on, the firmware that is stored in a EEPROM (electrically erasable programmable read-only memory) chip (BIOS or UEFI firmware) initializes the hardware, including the screen and keyboard, and tests the main memory.

1st stage boot loader - MBR takes control over BIOS and looks for an active parition (boot flag = 1)

2nd stage boot loader (Kernel boot loader) - Usually GRUB, but could be ISOLINUX for removable media, DAS U-Boot for embedded devices

Kernel into memory - using the root filesystems (initrd or initramfs) for Kernel modules (/ˈmɑːdʒuːl/) loading, hardware detection and device discovery

system and service manager - systemd or initd --> init process

Some theory! init is the first and final Linux user-level process (PID: 1), residing in /sbin/init/, a daemon that continues running until the system is shut down.
It starts and manages non-kernel processes: adopting orphaned child, and taking care of those who have terminated without an exit status (zombie or defunct process): this is way it's also called the zombie killer or child reaper. 

But... Zombies do happen to persist! What about 'em?

# find zombies by names 
# if you want their parents, then $ pstree -p -s [PID]
$ ps aux | grep 'Z'

# find zombies by PIDs
for pid in $(ps axo pid=,stat= | awk '$2~/^Z/ { print $1 }') ; do
    echo "$pid" # do something interesting here
done
How can we import a public key in a RPM-based system?
$ rpm --import https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc
This is how we can display a line of text, enabling the interpretation of backslash escapes. But what is the difference?

$ echo -e "You...\t bastards!"

$ printf "You... \t bastards!"
echo adds a \n at the end.

The high level package management systems automatically download packages and satisfy their dependencies. The underlying ones (low level) can only unpack, install, remove, and build packages. Let's translate from one type to the other.

# install or upgrade package
$ dpkg -i [file.deb]
$ rpm -Uvh [file.rpm]

# list installed packages
$ dpkg -l
$ rpm -qa

# find out which package installed a file
$ dpkg -S [file]
$ rpm -qf [file]

# install or upgrade package
$ aptitude update && aptitude install [package_name] 
$ yum update && yum install [package_name]

# list installed packages
$ aptitude search '~i!~M'
$ yum list installed

# find out which package installed a file
$ apt-file update && apt search [file]
$ yum whatprovides [file]
How show (interactively, keeping the file open) the first and last 5 lines of a log?

$ head -f -5 [file]

$ tail -f -5 [file]
By default, tail and head display 10 lines.
This is how we get the type of a file, including (if they are executables) the bit version (32 or 64) and (if they are text) the encoding. Now, suppose we want to change that encoding...
$ file -i [file]

# Persian subtitles anyone? iconv -f windows-1256 -t UTF-8 a-separation_FA.srt -o a-separation_FA_UTF8.srt
$ iconv -f [from-encoding] -t [to-encoding] [input] -o [output]
How can we show system hardware (including battery condition and memory), system software (drivers, desktop environment, kernel, distro), processes and RAM usage in one fell swoop?
$  inxi -Fm

Self-Monitoring, Analysis and Reporting Technology System (SMART) is built into most modern hard disks: a set of utilities that will provide advanced warning of disk status, degradation and failure. 

Now, how can we use it to get complete information (especially SSD health) on a disk drive (/dev/sda)?

And... Yes, there's a service for it that should be running! It's smartd

# Fast test - Look for (according to producer) SSD_Life_Left, Wear_Leveling_Count - 100 means 100% life etc
$ smartctl -a /dev/sda

# Longer test - Execution (launch it again to see progression) and results
$ smartctl -t long /dev/sda
$ smartctl -l selftest /dev/sda

# /etc/smartd.conf - These are the only lines that should not be commented
# Here we have two NVME devices (in RAID 1), with mail alerting
# Remember that $ lsblk -d is your friend in determining physical disks
/dev/nvme1n1 -d nvme -H -C 0 -U 0 -m hosting@my_domain.com
/dev/nvme0n1 -d nvme -H -C 0 -U 0 -m hosting@my_domain.com 

$ systemctl start smartd

GUI environment?

# Gnome - "Smart Data & Self Tests" in the general options
$ gnome-disks

# KDE - Device - "Smart Status"
$ partitionmanager
halt, poweroff and reboot are legacy commands for compatibility purpose. Nowadays, we can just use...

# shuts down at 10.00 with a wall message for all users
$ shutdown [-H | -P | -r]  10:00 "Shutting down for scheduled maintenance."

halt terminates all processes and shuts down the cpu; poweroff is exactly like halt, but it also turns off the unit itself (lights and everything on a PC). It sends an ACPI command to the board, then to the PSU, to cut the power.
This is how we print a calendar. Wagt about the current date and time, or maybe one month earlier/later?
$ cal [month] [year]

# common use case: YEAR-month-day-hour:minute:second Check "man date" for a complete list of possible formats
$ date "+%Y-%m-%d-%H:%M:%S"

# next or previous month number
$ date +%m -d "-1 month"
$ date +%m -d "+1 month"

We want to configure and enable a stratum 3 NTP server. That means...

- Connecting your server to the NTP pool (a virtual cluster of timeservers)
- Restart the daemon
- query the timeserver, by showing a list of peers (you may have to wait a few minutes)

### Debian Mode ###

# /etc/ntp.conf - format: server timeserver-address
server 0.pool.ntp.org
server 1.pool.ntp.org
server 2.pool.ntp.org
server 3.pool.ntp.org

# the package name is "ntp" and possibly also the service
$ systemctl start ntpd && systemctl enable ntpd

# check status
$ systemctl status ntpd
$ ntpq -p

# check that it's being used 
# you may have to reboot
$ timedatectl set-ntp true
$ timedatectl
$ date -R

### Photon-OS Mode ###
# /etc/systemd/timesyncd.conf
[Time]
NTP=0.pool.ntp.org 1.pool.ntp.org 2.pool.ntp.org 3.pool.ntp.org

$ systemctl restart systemd-networkd && systemctl restart systemd-timesyncd

Also make sure that you have:

- The right hardware clock time in $ hwclock - If not, get it right on the OS and then write it to BIOS by using $ hwclock -w
- The right locale when using $ dpkg-reconfigure tzdata
- The right timezone in /etc/timezone

Failed to create dbus connection when using $timedatectl? 
$ apt-get install dbus

Using a container? Try these flags with docker run:
--privileged --volume /run/dbus/system_bus_socket:/run/dbus/system_bus_socket:ro
How can we display information about an X server (e.g. your current resolution)?
$ xdpyinfo | grep dim

We can do it the HARD way, or we can do it the SOFT way...

- When the original copy is moved, deleted, renamed etc, a hard link will still point to the same contents - that is, the same inode,.y.
Must be in the same filesystem and cannot point to a directory!

- A soft (symbolic) link will only point to a given pathname.
Can cross filesystems and can point to a directory!

Let's create both and then remove 'em.

# going to a directory?! Add "/" to the destination!
# using relative paths? give the command from the TARGET directory
# yeah, you could use [link_to_create] but... why? easier this way
$ ln [-s] [destination_file] 

# this is enough and is safe, even if the target was a directory
$ rm [soft_link]

If the destination file does not exist, ln -s will still work!
BUT... make sure that you don't use "/" in the link_to_create (unless you want to be the symlink WITHIN a certain already existing directory)

Every file is associated with its own inode: a data structure on disk that describes and stores file attributes (permissions, user and group ownership, size, timestamps, last access time, last modification time). 

Linux filesystems use inode reservation: to improve performance, several inodes are reserved when a directory is created, expecting them to be used in the future when new files are created.

But the important and practical bit to know is that inodes can be depleted and thus break your system! Keep them in check, by getting into the habit of using... $ df -hTi and not just $ df -h! What about improving the sitatuation?
$ df -hTi

# find empty files and directories

$ find /home -type f -empty
$ find /home -type d -empty

# delete the bastards (use with caution!)
$ find /home -type f -empty --delete
$ find /home -type d -empty -- delete
How can we change the directory and push it into a stack, pop the first item of that stack and print the stack?

$ pushd [dir]
$ popd
$ dirs
BTW, remember that to simply go the previous directory(not necessarily the parent!), $ cd - is enough and for $HOME we can use $ cd without even that ~

This is how can we print a file (or a concatenation of files) to stdout from begging to end or viceversa. Common problems:

- Suppose that the first filenames is "-", which cat ot tac will treat as a synonym for stdin. To get around this...

- We want to see the different filenames at the beginning of each output
$ [cat | tac] [file_1] [file_n]

$ [cat | tac] [./file_1] [file_n]

$ tail -n+1 [file_1] [file_n]

Prefix the filename with a path. This technique is also used to get around similar issues where command line options clash with filenames, so a file referred to as ./-e does not appear as the -e command line option to a program, for example.
What are file descriptors (file streams)?

stdin is file descriptor 0, stdout (terminal screen) is file descriptor 1, and stderr is file descriptor 2. 

Typically, if other files are opened in addition to these three, which are opened by default, they will start at file descriptor 3 and increase from there.
What are core files?
Files which contain diagnostic information after a program fails.
Find files according to their name (regex pattern=? This is fast and dirty , but we long long listing format.

# fast and dirty (fast and dirty approach)
$ find . | grep -E [pattern]

# you don't need -R on the ls
$ ls -l $(locate -r [pattern])

# don't forget the "/" at the end of the directory name, or it won't be recursive
$ find [dir1/] [dir2/] -iname [*pattern*] [-ls]

$ locate uses a prebuilt database to find both files and directories according to their names. Very fast, but can be inaccurate if the database is not updated (see updatedb card). We can use with $ ls -l to get "long listing" information.

$ find iterates in real time over a filesystem. You can filter by any attribute and also execute a command on the files.
What is the difference between RAID 01 and RAID 1+0?

RAID 01 (mirror of stripes), achieving both replication and sharing of data between disks. Minimum disks: 4

RAID 1+0 (stripe of mirrors). It is the preferable RAID level for I/O-intensive applications needing data safeness too, such as big data database. Minimum disks: 4. Very costly (capacity is half!). Better performance than RAID 5 or 6 (no parity strings).
How to create a RAID device?

$ mdadm --create --verbose /dev/md0 --level=stripe --raid-devices=2 /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1

$ mdadm --create --verbose /dev/md0 --level=1 --raid-devices=2 /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1

$ mdadm --create --verbose /dev/md0 --level=5 --raid-devices=3 /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1 /dev/sdd1 --spare-devices=1 /dev/sde1

$ mdadm --create --verbose /dev/md0 --level=6 --raid-devices=4 /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1 /dev/sdd1 /dev/sde --spare-devices=1 /dev/sdf1

$ mdadm --create --verbose /dev/md0 --level=10 --raid-devices=4 /dev/sd[b-e]1 --spare-devices=1 /dev/sdf1
You should already know that to correctly use locate, the database must be updated, and you can do it by hand (see code below). But suppose it still doesn't find what you're looking for...

# BTW, you can do it even without being root!
$ updatedb --require-visibility 0 -o ~/.locate.db
# Look for PRUNENAMES, PRUNEPATHS or PRUNEFS in /etc/updatedb.conf
BTW, PRUNENAMES might be useful to filter out .git or .svn directories!
How can we take a screenshot, delaying the action by 10 seconds, supposing you're in a Gnome environment?
$ gnome-screenshot -d 10
You tried $ man [cmd] and found out that the command is a shell built-in. To read it easily...

# -m is to output in a pseudo-manpage format
$ help -m [cmd]

Sometimes, these commands have the same name as executable programs on 
the system. Thus, slightly different behavior can be expected from the 
built-in version of a command such as echo as compared to /bin/echo.

You're in the middle of a certification exam and you...

- Don't remember a command

- Want to know how to use it, considering that man pages are usually useless (https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/306189/why-dont-man-pages-have-examples/306303)

# also man -k [string]
$ apropos [string]

$ yum install ruby-devl gcc-c++ gem
$ gem install bropages
$ bro [cmd]
What is the difference between a process with threads of executions (tasks) and a multi-threaded process?

Threads are "light weight" process that run under the umbrella of a main "heavy weight" process, sharing some resources (memory, open files, signal handlers), but scheduled and run by the system on an individual basis.

When all resources are shared, we talk of a multi-threaded process.
What are PID, PPID, PGID, TID?

Process ID: Unique Process ID number

Parent Process ID: Process (Parent) that started this process. 

PGID: Process Group ID

Thread ID (TID): This is the same as the PID for single-threaded processes. For a multi-threaded process, each thread shares the same PID, but has a unique TID.

Typical exam questions. How can we...?

- Print newlines or word counts for a text file

- Numbers all non-empty lines

# exclude empty lines? | sed '/^\s*#/d;/^\s*$/d'
$ wc -[l|w] [file]

$ nl [file]
What does the third line of top output show?

- user (us), system (sy) and altered niceness (ni) processes

- Idle mode (id), that should be low if the load average is high, and vice versa

- Jobs waiting for I/O (wa)

- Hardware interrupts (hi) and software interrupts (si)

- Steal Time

All the values are percentages of the CPU time.
How can we schedule a command (non-interactive) to be executed in the future (2 days from now)? How can we see the at queue?

$ at now + 2 days

$ atq
Crontab basics

# add a new crontab
# you'll probably use something like > /dev/null 2>&1
# as an alternative, you could add a script to /etc/cron.daily etc
$ crontab -e

# verify the current crontab for a certain user
# your own? just omit -u [user]
$ crontab -u [user] -l

# there could also be /var/log/cron or /var/log/messages
$ grep [script] /var/log/syslog

# test before time
# this implies adding the script to /etc/cron.daily etc
# you gotta have the rights to execute or it shall fail silently
# remove --test to actually execute it
$ run-parts -v --test /etc/cron.hourly
- Pure Gold: https://crontab.guru

The load average is a gauge of how many processes are, on average, concurrently demanding CPU attention. It takes into account processes that are: running, runnable, uninterruptible sleepers. How should we read this line, from programs like $ top or $ w?

0.45, 0.17, 0.12

Last minute --> CPU utilization has been 45% of capacity (average)
Last 5 minutes --> CPU utilization has been 17% of capacity (average)
Last 15 minutes --> CPU utilization has been 12% of capacity (average)

A load average of 1.00 per CPU indicates a fully subscribed system. If the load average goes above this value (overloaded system), it indicates that processes are competing for CPU time and there could be a problem, such as a runaway process (non-responding).

- BUT... Take into account multiple cores! Dual-core? Worry when load average > 2.00. Other sources suggest a 1.5 value for core, so worry when a dual core is > 3.00

- On top, press "1" to see the different cores
The sane's way to diff. We want to: only report difference (ignoring whitespace) and have a side-by-side comparison
$ diff -yw --suppress-common-lines [file1] [file2]
Ignore case? Add -i

dd is used to copy a file, converting and formatting according to the operands. Often, people joke that it means disk destroyer! Now, let's use it to backup and restore the MRB, and thus the partition table.

# backup
$ dd if=/dev/sda of=sda.mbr bs=512 count=1

# restore
$ dd if=sda.mbr of=/dev/sda bs=512 count=1
The block size (bs) is a unit measuring the number of bytes that are read and written at one time from the input file (if) to the output file (of), while count copies only this number of blocks.
Time to diff, that is to generate patches.

# compare two files with a "unified output"
$ diff --color -u [file1] [file2] > [path/to/patchfile]

# compare two directories trees recursively
# -N forces files which have been added or deleted to appear in the differencing ( = treat as empty), instead of just generating a warning
# -w skips trailing spaces
$ diff --color -Nurw [old] [new] > [path/to/patchfile]

# compare 3 files
$ diff3 [file1] [file2] [file3] > [path/to/patchfile]

# apply a generated patch
# -p1 means the patch was made while standing one directory up
$ patch -p1 < [/path/to/pathfile]

Unified Output:

- The --- notes the first file and +++ the second file
- The @@ line gives the line number context for both files
- Lines that have been removed in going from file1 to file2 are denoted by - and lines that have been added are denoted by +

Btw, once you have files to compare two by two, use diffuse!

Let's talk compression algorithms. From the most space-wasting to the least (conversely, also from faster to slower):

gzip - Uses the DEFLATE algorithm, which is a combination of Huffman and Lempel-Ziv (LZ77) coding.

bzip2 - Uses Burrows-Wheeler algorithm and Huffman coding.

xz - Uses the Lempel–Ziv–Markov algorithm (LZMA).

How can we convert from one to the other?

# https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/308685
find . -name '*.gz' -type f -exec bash -o pipefail -Cc '
  for file do
    gunzip < "$file" | xz > "${file%.gz}.xz" && rm -f "$file"
  done' bash {} +
How can we compress a directory with a bzip2 algorithm and then decompress it, in both cases keeping the original files?

$ tar cjf [archive.tar.bz2] [dir] # bzip2 compresses files only; use tar to pack a directory first

# equivalent to bzip2 -dk
$ tar xjf [archive.tar.bz2] # decompress + extract
How are UIDs assigned?

UID 0 is reserved for root user

UID's ranging from 1-999 are reserved for system accounts and groups

Normal users have UID's of 1000 or greater
Let's say you did some deep customizing shit (like .bashrc and the like) and want every new user to have that booty. Then...
By default all files from /etc/skel are copied to the new user’s home directory; go thar and edit.

Let's do a group thing.

- Create a group and verify its creation

- Assign an user to this group and verify the change

- Assign to this group the ownership of a directory and verify the change

- Delete the group

$ groupadd [group]
$ getent group | grep [group]

$ usermod -aG [group] [user]
$ id [user] # $ groups would require to log out and in, first

# equivalent to chown :[group]
$ chgrp [group] [dir]
$ stat [dir]

$ groupdel [group]

-G is to supply supplementary (secondary) groups which the user is also a member of. If the user is currently a member of a secondary group which is not listed, the user will be removed from the group. This behaviour can be changed via the -a option, which appends the user to the current supplementary group list.
Before resetting an environment variable (like $PS1), we should...
$ OLD_PS1=$PS1
Save the old one (to restore it, if needed)
You're in Carcosa, now... Well, you're in /var/log and you have both compressed (gz) and uncompressed files to grep! What about it?
$ zcat -f [pattern]
Goldie gold!

sed (stream editor) takes data from an input stream that is taken and moved to a working stream (space). Then a list of operations is applied over the data  and the final contents are moved to the standard output stream.

Now, let's replace. Hard. 

- Given a list of telephone numbers of the form 123456789 use sed to rewrite and pretty print 'em as (123)456-789 -

- Break a ROT13 cipher

- Select and convert all file names with suffix .html given as output by ls into capital letters with suffix .HTM (solution with and without GNU extensions)

- Use sed to extract full user names (5th field) from /etc/passwd/

# basic syntax (the [flag] is tipically "g", that is global replace)
# to avoid crazy escaping, use colons (:) instead of slashes (/) when the slash is part of the expression
# the whole 's/[term]/[replacement]/[flag]' could be repeated multiple times (more than one patterns) with the -e flag
$ sed 's/[term]/[replacement]/[flag]' [file]

# regex with capture group:  The non standard -E option is to avoid escaping parenthesis
$ sed -E 's/(.*)\.png/\1.jpg/g' [file]

# We need \( ... \) for the regex
$ sed 's/^\([[:digit:]]\{3\}\)\([[:digit:]]\{3\}\)\([[:digit:]]\{3\}\)$/(\1)\2-\3/g' numeri.txt

# just look for the list in Wikipedia

$ sed -n 's/^\([^:]*:\)\{4\}\([^:,]\+\)[^:]*:.*$/\2/p' /etc/passwd

The Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol is a standardized network protocol used on Internet Protocol (IP) networks. The DHCP is controlled by a DHCP server that dynamically distributes network configuration parameters, such as IP addresses, for interfaces and services. A router or a residential gateway can be enabled to act as a DHCP server. 

- A new host is connected to the network: it sends a DHCP Discovery broadcast packet using UDP on port 67. Since the host still needs an IP to be assigned, the source address of the packet is 0.0.0.0.

- The DHCP server in the same broadcast domain responds with a DHCP Offer packet. The field YIADDR contains the "Your IP address". In other words it is populated with the IP address the server is offering to the client.
The DST IP (destination IP address) of a DHCP offer packet is still broadcast and the Lease Time (in seconds) defines the validity period of the offered IP.

- The client responds with another broadcast packet: DHCP REQUEST. The destination address is still broadcast since more than one DHCP server may have send a DHCPOFFER. DHCP clients choose the best offer according to the lease time attribute in the DHCP offer: the longer the better

- The DHCP server that recognizes itself as the winner sends a DHCPACK packet in broadcast. The YIADDR contains the client IP address while the CHADDR (Client Ethernet Address) contains the client MAC address.

We want to create a sudoer (user that can escalate to root) and make the motherfucker able to sudo any command without being prompted for a password.

# /etc/sudoers.d/student
# beware! the filename cannot have dots, so "student.01" won't be read! happened!
# user student, in ALL hosts, can run commands with the privilege of (ALL) - alias any - other user and execute (without a password) ALL commands
student ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL

# allow to execute a single command only?
student ALL = (root) NOPASSWD: /path/to/my/program

# Some distributions may require 400 instead of 440
$ chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/student 

# ~/student/.bashrc
# Otherwise the directories /sbin and /usr/sbin are not searched, since sudo inherits the PATH of the user, not the full root user
PATH=$PATH:/usr/sbin:/sbin 

# check new permissions
$ su - [student]
$ sudo -l

- Also make sure that sudo is actually installed on the server!

- You'll probably also need to log out and in to make changes effective.

- We could use $ visudo (enabling syntax checking and lock!) to edit /etc/sudoers, but this is a more modern solution... And no, when using visudo we don't need to uncomment # includedir /etc/sudoers.d because the "#" is part of the directive!

The system maintains a so-called entropy pool of digital numbers/random bits. The Linux kernel offers the /dev/random and /dev/urandom device nodes, which draw on the entropy pool to provide random numbers. What is the difference?

/dev/random is used where very high quality randomness is required, such as one-time pad or key generation, but it is relatively slow to provide vaules. When the entropy pool is empty, /dev/random is blocked and does not generate any number until additional environmental noise (network traffic, mouse movement, etc.) is gathered.

/dev/urandom is faster and suitable (good enough) for most cryptographic purposes. It can reuse the internal pool to produce more pseudo-random bits.

POSIX IPC involves three mechanisms: Shared Memory Segments; Semaphores;  Message Queues. Anyway, there are still plenty of System V IPC applications found in the wild. 

- Get an overall summary of System V IPC activity on your system and look for leaks

-  Gain further information about the processes that have created shared memory segments and processes last attached to that segments

- Supposing a line like 1441811 (Shared Memory ID) coop (owner) 2048 (Creator PID) 20573 (Last Operator PID), how can inspect what do 2048 and 20573 correspond to?

# The second key has no attachments (nattch = 0) and the status is not marked for destruction (dest). It's a leak!
$ ipcs

$ ipcs -p

$ ps aux | grep -e 20573 -e 2048
Note almost all of the currently running shared memory segments have a key of 0x0000000 (also known as IPC_PRIVATE) which means they are only shared between processes in a parent/child relationship.
What is the difference between a windowing system, a window manager and a display manager?

Windowing system: windows for applications to draw in. The X Window System is standard on Unix systems - now being replaced by Wayland - and X.Org is its standard implementation. X(11) server manages the display hardware and X(11) clients are the applications, communicating through the X11 protocol.

Window manager: an X client (ˈklaɪənt/) that receives notifications when new windows are created and typically traps a number of user events (key and mouse bindings). Its job is to choose where to display windows and at what size, to show and hide windows, to display window decorations (borders, title bar, …). Mutter for Gnome, or KWin for KDE.

Display manager: It loads the X server, handles graphical logins, and starts the appropriate desktop environment after a user logs in (display managment). gdm (Gnome), kdm (KDE). Called by $ startx
By $ kill -l we can see all possible signals. The default is SIGTERM (15), a terminate signal that can be handled. There are also two terminate signals that cannot be handled...
SIGKILL (9) and SIGSTOP (19)
This is the how we kill all processes with a given name (bash) or PID, just part of a name, owner user or group owner.

# PIDs is more likely to succeed... Use pidof [process_name] when in doubt
$ pkill -c -9 [PID | process_name]

$ pkill -c -f [part_of_process_name]

$ pkill -c -9 -u [username]

$ pkill -c -9 -G [GID]

- The -9 is equivalent to -SIGKILL

- There is also a killall, but "pkill is the one that's worth teaching future generations" (https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/91527/whats-the-difference-between-pkill-and-killall)

- We use pkill as a wrapper for the kill command (itself a wrapper to the kill() syscall), that would onl works with PIDs

- We always use -c, because counting the matching processes it's the only way to have a "verbose" pkill
What's the difference between SIGSTOP and SIGTSTP?

SIGSTOP is a signal sent programmatically (eg: kill -STOP pid ) and cannot be ignored.

SIGTSTP is typically sent by a user typing on a keyboard, usually Control-Z, and might be ignored.

BTW, both are designed to suspend a process which will be eventually resumed with SIGCONT

Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) is the automatic protocol for managing the mappings between IP addresses (Layer 3) and MAC addresses (Layer 2). How does it work?

# Show ARP Table
$ arp

1. Host S checks its ARP Table (ARP Cache) to see whether the MAC address for the destination IP is already known. Entries are valid for 2–10 seconds and are continuously refreshed by the protocol.

2. If the MAC is not known, S sends an ARP Request as a broadcast (at Layer 2), asking which host owns that IP.

3. When host X receives the request, it ignores it if the IP is not its own; otherwise it sends S an ARP Reply in unicast containing its MAC address.

4. S can now encapsulate the packet in an Ethernet II frame, send it over the network, and store the result in its ARP Table.

What should be the minimum size of a root partition (separate from a /home partition)? What about a swap partition?

Root Partition: 
As of 2016, the consensus seems to be around 25 GB: https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/128442/why-should-we-use-a-15-25-gb-root-partition

Swap Partition:

- On a single disk system, try to center a single swap partition; on multiple disk systems, try to spread swap partitions over disks

- At minimum it should equal your physical memory (RAM) size if you use hibernation, otherwise you need a minimum of round(sqrt(RAM)) and a maximum of twice the amount of RAM. See table here: https://help.ubuntu.com/community/SwapFaq#How_much_swap_do_I_need.3F

- In order to determine if a server has sufficient swap space, monitor the 'Swap' line in the output of the `free` command. If the amount of free swap space approaches zero, you may have insufficient swap space. Diminishing returns: if you need more swap space than twice your RAM size, you'd better add more RAM!

Some CLI basics in PDF manipulation

# Reduce the size of a PDF file
$ gs -sDEVICE=pdfwrite -dCompatibilityLevel=1.4 -dPDFSETTINGS=/screen -dNOPAUSE -dQUIET -dBATCH -dDetectDuplicateImages -dCompressFonts=true -r150 -sOutputFile=[output.pdf] [input.pdf]

# Merge (unify, join, concatenate, unite) multiple PDFs file into a single one (cat)
# in a hurry? just use $ pdftk [input.pdf] cat output [output.pdf]
$ gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -q -sDEVICE=pdfwrite -dPDFSETTINGS=/screen -sOutputFile=[merged.pdf] [input1.pdf] [input2.pdf]

# Search a pattern (recursively, also in sub-directories)
$ pdfgrep -R "pattern" [*.pdf]

# Just keep a range of pages

# Darken it, if the contrast is too low

$ convert output*.jpg -normalize -threshold 80% final-%02d.jpg
$ convert final*.jpg my_new_highcontrast.pdf

# File quality changes according to...

    -dPDFSETTINGS=/ebook for better quality, but slightly larger pdfs.
    -dPDFSETTINGS=/prepress output similar to Acrobat Distiller "Prepress Optimized" setting
    -dPDFSETTINGS=/printer selects output similar to the Acrobat Distiller "Print Optimized" setting
    -dPDFSETTINGS=/default selects output intended to be useful across a wide variety of uses, possibly at the expense of a larger output file

# To use ImageMagick on PDFS, first make sure there are the rights permissions to PDF files in /etc/ImageMagick-6/policy.xml
# You could have ImageMagick-7 etc according to version
# By default, it's probably set the "none"

$ gs -sOutputFile=[output.pdf] -sDEVICE=pdfwrite -sColorConversionStrategy=Gray -dProcessColorModel=/DeviceGray -dCompatibilityLevel=1.4 -dNOPAUSE -dBATCH -dAutoRotatePages=/None [input.pdf]

Let's talk performance monitoring. The utilities you should know...

### Nmon (this should be your first choice!!) ###
$ nmon

### Systems Activity Reporter (sar) ###,

# I/O and transfer rate statistic: transactions (total, read, write) and blocks (read, write) / second
$ sar -b

# block device activity
$ sar -x

### IoStat  (see notes) ###

# result in MB
# We could also use -N to show by device name (root, home, swap)
$ iostat -xm

### VmStat ###
$ vmstat

iostat is the basic workhorse utility for monitoring I/O device activity on the system. After a brief summary of CPU utilization, I/O statistics are given: tps (I/O transactions per second), blocks read and written per unit time, and the total blocks read and written. Information is broken out by disk partition and if LVM is being used also by dm (device mapper) logical partitions.

Rules of Thumb:
- %idle (time) must be greater than %iowait (time)
- A system can be considered as I/O-bound when the CPU is waiting for I/O to complete, that means the %iowait time is approaching 100 percent

• Fork off 6 memory-intensive processes, each spinning on malloc(), allocating 256 MB by default

• Run the stress test for 20 seconds
$ stress -c 8 -i 4 -m 6 -t 20s

The code below shows how to manually mount and umount (even if unreachable) network shares SMB/CIFS (for Windows clients) as part of the local filesystem. Now:

- Set up persistent mounting

- Instruct systemd to re-read the /etc/fstab

# -t [cifs | nfs] is optional!
$ mount -o [user,pass] [//SERVER/share] /media/samba

# -- lazy: detach and clean references to the fs
$ umount -lf /media/samba

# see Samba shares
$ smbclient -L 192.168.0.10

# /etc/fstab
//SERVER/share /media/samba cifs creds=/root/smbfile,defaults 0  0

# /root/smbfile
username=student
password=student
$ chmod 600 /root/smbfile

# as suggested by /etc/fstab itself!
$ systemctl daemon-reload

- \040 replaces any space in the server path

- fs_freq and fs_passno are usually set to 0

- Needed packages: samba-client, samba-common, cifs-utils

- $ smbclient is for temporary access, but of course you can $ mount -t cifs the share, use /etc/fstab or automount

# -T: rename if the DEST doesn't exist or it's empty, and handles capitalization! 
$ mv -T [old] [new]
This is how can we recursively change directory-only or file-only permissions. Let's comment on that
$ find /path -type [d|f] -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755

-print0 is to print the full file name on the standard output, followed by a null character. This allows file names that contain newlines or other types of white space to be correctly interpreted by programs that process the find output.

-0 input items are terminated by a null character. Useful when input items might contain white space, quote marks, or backslashes. The GNU find -print0 option produces input suitable for this mode.

The ps command utility has existed in all UNIX-like operating system variants, and that diversity is reflected in the complicated potpourri of options that the Linux version of ps accepts, which fall into three categories...

UNIX options, which must be preceded by -, and which may be grouped.

    BSD options, which must NOT be preceded by -, and which may be grouped.

    GNU long options, each of which must be preceded by --.

Most system administrators tend to use one or two standard combinations for their daily use.
In traditional BIOS systems, the Master Boot Record (MBR), alias the boot sector, or first-stage boot loader, is...

The first 512-byte sector on the disk that contains:

- 446 bytes reserved for program code. They typically hold part of a boot loader program (second-stage boot loader: GRUB).

- 64 bytes for the partition table, with each entry (16 bytes) having: beginning and ending address in cylinder/head/sectors (CHS) format, partition type code, start sector and number of sectors in partition. ​​​​​​​Linux only uses the last two fields for addressing using the linear block addressing (LBA) method.

- 2 final bytes known as the magic number, signature word, or end of sector marker, which always have the value 0x55AA: a validation check of the MBR.
What is the difference between resident set size (RSS) and virtual size (VSZ)?

RSS is how much memory this process currently has in main memory (RAM). 

VSZ is how much virtual memory the process has in total: this includes all types of memory, both in RAM and swapped out.
What is the difference between active and inactive memory pages?

active: have been recently used; they may be clean (disk contents are up to date) or dirty (need to be flushed to disk eventually). 

inactive: have not been recently used and are more likely to be clean and are released sooner under memory pressure.
How can we set a default entry in the GRUB boot loader?

# /etc/default/grub - can be edited by hand
# zero-based index is that of the entry in the file above
GRUB_DEFAULT=0

# Fedora / CentOS
$ grub2-mkconfig -o [file above]

# Debian-based
$ update-grub

What is the difference between /dev/sr0 and /dev/cdrom?

sr0 is the first SCSI CD-ROM device in the system, but this may be misleading, as SCSI and SATA are interchangeable in Linux terminology.

cdrom is usually a symlink to the one above.
What is the Virtual File System (VFS) abstraction layer?
Files and their names - camouflaging the Physical I/O Layer. Thus, it allows to "ignore" (handle transparently) drives, partitions (physical or logical) and [network] filesystems too.
What is a journalling filesystem?

A filesystem able to recover from system crashes or ungraceful shutdowns with little or no corruption, and do so very rapidly. Operations are grouped into transactions. A transaction must be completed without error, atomically; otherwise, the filesystem is not changed.

# yes... https://serverfault.com/questions/797044/openssh-adding-an-ssh-key-from-putty-to-authorized-keys

Should we use sudo or gksudo / kdesudo?

You should never use normal sudo to start graphical applications as Root. You should use gksudo (kdesudo on Kubuntu) to run such programs. gksudo sets HOME=~root, and copies .Xauthority to a tmp directory. This prevents files in your home directory becoming owned by Root.

Note (2018+): gksudo has been removed from modern Ubuntu/Debian. Use pkexec or configure sudoers with the DISPLAY and XAUTHORITY variables. For most cases, running sudo -E app with caution is the practical alternative.

Linux permits the system to overcommit memory, so that it can grant memory requests that exceed the size of RAM + Swap. While this might seem foolhardy, many (if not most) processes (loaded in RAM or Swap) do not use all requested memor.

    0:  (default) Permit overcommission, but refuse obvious overcommits, and give root users somewhat more memory allocation than normal users.

    1:  All memory requests are allowed to overcommit.

    2:  Turn off overcommission. Memory requests will fail when the total memory commit reaches the size of the swap space plus a configurable percentage (50 by default) of RAM. This factor is modified changing /proc/sys/vm/overcommit_ratio

Redis best practices (https://redis.io/topics/admin) require to change it by default. How?

# persistent - /etc/sysctl.conf
vm.overcommit_memory = 1 to

# real time
sysctl vm.overcommit_memory=1
What is steal time?
Percentage of time a virtual CPU (machine) waits for a real CPU while the hypervisor is servicing another virtual processor.

Let's talk about dynamic hostname resolution: we want to provide several DNS servers (max 3) for resolver lookups. BTW, this is how to check.

To know how to flush caches there's another card

# don't use these to check! this will only show the local DNS cache
$ nslookup [domain.com]
$ dig [domain.com]

# the first command is equivalent to resolvectl status | grep -i -A1 "DNS Server"
# the second command should have the same result, but is only useful if you're using NetworkManager
$ resolvectl | grep -A "DNS Server"
$ ( nmcli dev list || nmcli dev show ) 2>/dev/null | grep DNS

# assuming the "resolvconf" service is being used (check with sudo systemctl is-active resolvconf)
# /etc/resolvconf/resolv.conf.d/[base | head | tail] - in our experience "tail"
nameserver 8.8.8.8
$ resolvconf -u

# assuming the "NetworkManager" service is being used  (check with sudo systemctl is-active NetworkManager)
$ nmcli con mod [connectionName] ipv4.dns "8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4"
$ nmcli con mod [connectionName] ipv4.ignore-auto-dns yes
$ nmcli con down [connectionName] && nmcli con up [connectionName]

# still, the real boss is probably the "systemd-resolve" service! Nowadays it's the one controlling /etc/resolv.conf
# /etc/systemd/resolved.conf
# the last two lines are necessary to make the changes immediately
[Resolve]
DNS=8.8.8.8
$ service systemd-resolved restart
$ systemd-resolve --status
$ rm -f /etc/resolv.conf 
$ ln -s /run/systemd/resolve/resolv.conf /etc/resolv.conf

To prevent shit like the shell fork bomb (that would launch two instances of a function, and then duplicate each of them in a neverending loop), we need to place some fucking limits. We have soft limits - which can change quite freely - but never beyond hard limits. Both can be temporary (using $ ulimit, but that's very limited in usage) or persistent.

Let's set an hard limit - different for a group of user and for root - of the maximum number of processes a user can start.

# /etc/security/limits.conf for persistence - well commented, no need to remember!
@student hard nproc 20
root hard nproc 1000
We could do exactly the same for open file descriptors (= max number of open files), by using nofile instead of noproc.

There are conditions under which the RPM database stored in /var/lib/rpm can be corrupted. We will construct a new one and verify its integrity.

1. Backup the contents of /var/lib/rpm as the rebuild process will overwrite the contents! DO IT!

2.  Rebuild the database.

3.  Get a printed to screen and saved listing of all rpms on the system.

# -a (archive mode) includes the -recursive flag and preserves SELinux attributes, links, xattr, etc. There are better tools for making a backup, but if you're using cp for a backup, don't use anything other than cp -a!!!
$ cp -a rpm rpm_BACKUP

$ rpm --rebuilddb

$ rpm -qa | tee /tmp/rpm-qa.output

Let's talk processes' priority.

- Run ps to show nice value of processes

- Start a certain process (Bash) using the nice command. What will happen to the priority of the process?

- Change the nice value of one of a process (an existing Bash session) using renice. What happens to the priority now?

# Bash for Windows: ps -o pid,nice,comm,stat
$ ps -o pid,pri,ni,cmd,stat

# The nice level is relative (i.e. less or more than before) 
$ nice -n 10 [PID]

# The nice level is absolute
$ renice 15 -p 22171

We want to monitor process state. So...

- Start a background process which reads from /dev/urandom and writes to /dev/null and then check the process state. What should it be?

- Bring the process to the foreground. How should the status change?

- Stop it. How should the status change?

- List the processes in the background (both stopped or running).

# State should be "R" (running / runnable / in run queue)
$ dd if=/dev/urandom of=/dev/null &

# We could select by PID with -p
$ ps -C dd -o pid,cmd,stat

#  After fg, state should be "R+"
$ fg

# Press Ctrl + Z. State should be "T" - stopped
# always show PID, in case you need to kill it
$ jobs -psl

If available memory is exhausted, Linux invokes the OOM (Out Of Memory) Killer / Reaper to decide which process(es) should be exterminated to open up some memory. Now, we want to invoke the OOM Killer. Let's:

- Examine what swap partitions and files are present on your system and turn off all swap (later, turn it back on again!)

- Put the system under increasing memory pressure, with a 10 seconds timeout

- You can see the OOM (Out of Memory) killer swoop in and try to kill processes in a struggle to stay alive by monitoring new output on...

- Turn swap back on.

$ cat /proc/swaps

$ /sbin/swapoff -a

[...]

$ /sbin/swapon -a

# 8 workers spinning on malloc() / free()
$ stress -v -m 8 -t 10s

# According to distribution... Remeber that you can specify multiple files at once!
$ tail -f [/var/log/messages | /var/log/syslog] | grep -i oom
How can we know which distribution version are we running?

# /etc/lsb-release - at least in Debian - could be not updated and you might need to edit it manually: https://askubuntu.com/questions/49040/apt-could-not-find-a-distribution-template-error
$ cat /etc/*release*

# no results?
$ cat /proc/version

Let's talk block devices and IO schedulers. We want to:

- List all block devices

- Check if one of them (sda) is a SSD

- Check its IO scheduler, change it to cfq and check its tunables

$ ls -l /sys/block

# 1 true (SSD), 0 false (HDD)
$ cat /sys/block/sda/queue/rotational

# usually, cfq is used for SSD and deadline for HDD
$ cat /sys/block/sda/queue/scheduler
$ echo cfq > /sys/block/sda/queue/scheduler

$ cat /sys/block/sda/queue/iosched

Among I/O Scheduler Tunables, we see the parameter HZ as a kernel-configured quantity, that corresponds to the number of jiffies per second, which the kernel uses as a coarse measure of time. Without getting into detail, let us just point out that 1 HZ=
1 second

The filesystem tmpfs - usually mounted at as ram disk (ram drive) at /dev/shm, which is a good place to create temporary files in memory - adjusts its size dynamically, up to the maximum it was mounted with (eventually filling not the RAM but swapspace); has methods for dealing with files and I/O that are optimized for speed and there is no need to pre-format the filesystem with a mkfs command. Now:

- Find all existing instances of tmpfs (also showing disk space usage)

- Mount a new instance of tmpfs (1 GB) anywhere on your directory structure. Excellent use case: https://wp-rocket.me/blog/mount-folder-ram/

$ df -h | grep tmpfs

$ mount -t tmpfs -o size=1G none /mnt/tmpfs

- Without the size option, the alloted size will be half the RAM by default.

- The device specification is necessary (otherwise, you'll get a "Can't find in /etc/fstab error).  none is a customary choice, but maybe not a fortunate one: the error message `none busy' from umount can be confusing.

- Do not forget to unmount when you are done with!

In the MBR era (before 2010) drives were divided into primary partitions and logical partitions - that is, physically contiguous groups of sectors. 

Partitions 1-4 are primary: on the first drive, /dev/sda1 is the first primary partition and /dev/sda2 is the second, etc. Of the primary partitions, one and only one can be designated as an extended partition, which can be subdivided further into logical partitions. 

Partitions 5-15 (SCSI and related standards, such as SATA, support up to 15 partitions on the disk) are logical partitions: if we created an extended partition as /dev/sda3 it could then be divided into logical partitions, with number designations like /dev/sda5, /dev/sda6 etc.

In the GPT era (after 2010)...

There's no more the distinction between primary and extended partitions, but GPT supports up to 128 (by default; that limit can be raised) partitions. Some tools refer to these as "primary partitions," although in the absence of extended and logical partition types, the designation "primary" is meaningless. 

Partition size can be up to the order of petabytes, whereas the maximum size for MBR partitions is 2 TB.

Drive designation using device node form is based on the ID number of the SCSI controllers. Suppose this case;

- Controller 0 has drives with ID #3 and #1

- Controller 1 has drives with ID #2 and #5

Then:

ID 1 would be /dev/sda
ID 3 would be /dev/sdb
ID 2 would be /dev/sdc
ID 5 would be /dev/sdd

BUT... Changing the port in which the disk is connected to (or attaching the disk to another server) can result in a different name! We could use labels - assigned by filesystem-specific utilities (see card), but we're all modern and stuff, thus we use UUIDs when mounting.

# show current UUIDS
$ lsblk -fm

# UUID
$ mount -U 26d58ee2-9d20-4dc7-b6ab-aa87c3cfb69a /home
We have two commands and we want to verify which one is the fastest one - that is to measure the execution time of a command. How?

$ time [cmd1 +params]

$ time [cmd2 +params]
What is the wheel group?
A special user group used on some Unix systems to control access to the sudo command, which allows a user to masquerade as another user (usually the super user).

We want to partition a disk or imagefile, using fdisk or gdisk.

- Create a disk label (alias partition table, thus making unusable any previous data!)

- Create a new primary partition and make it 256 MB (or whatever size you would like), and then another one

- Check the partition table

- Write the partition table to disk. All changes up to this point are temporary.

- Inform the OS of the partition table change

# -C 130: number of phony cylinders in the drive; only necessary in older versions (like RHEL 6), but will do no harm on other distributions
$ fdisk -C 130 imagefile

# 'o' for DOS-type MBR or 'g' for GPT partition table

# First and second primary:
 
# Check parition table

# Write

$ partprobe

We could then associate it with the first free loop device. Then, simply mount the single partitions, visible by $ ls -l /dev/loop1*
$ losetup -Pf --show imagefile

- According to the theory, fdisk is for MBR and gdisk for GPT... Truth be told, they both manage both.

Let's experiment some more with loop devices - making plain files accessible as block devices.

- Show the status of all loop devices

- Associate an imagefile (or partition) with the first free loop device, and show the chosen one. This means being able to mount the imagefile, without having to use mount -o loop

- Remove the association

# /dev/zero provides as many null characters (ASCII NUL, 0x00) as are read from it
$ dd if=/dev/zero of=imagefile bs=1M count=1024

# imagefile is not a block special device. Proceed anyway? (y,n) y
$ mkfs.ext4 -L [label] imagefile

$ losetup -a

# probably /dev/loop1
# this means we don't need $ mount -o loop imagefile /mnt/somewhere
$ losetup -f --show imagefile

# IMPORTANT: if you have mounted the loop device, please umount before detaching!
$ losetup -d /dev/loop1
Need to detach all? Simply do $ losetup -D

AFTER unmounting a partition filesystem and making sure nothing is writing to it (you probably want to mask + stop Docker first and also systemctl stop udev) 

- Check an unmounted partition filesystem

- Repair it without asking anyhting

- What's the difference if we're talking about a KVM guest (qcow file)?

$ fsck [/dev/something]

$ fsck -yD [/dev/something]

# KVM guest? Is it the root partion? Then, from the host machine
$ modprobe nbd max_part=8
$ virsh destroy [virtual-machine]
$ qemu-nbd --connect=/dev/nbd0 [/var/lib/libvirt/images/virtual-machine.qcow2]
$ fsck -yD [/dev/nbd0p1]
$ qemu-nbd --disconnect [/dev/nbd0]
$ virsh start virtual-machine

# Not the root partition? Then just umount + fsck as above from the guest itself
If a pre-existing directory is used and it contains files prior to being used as a mount point, they will be...
hidden after mounting. These files are not deleted and will again be visible when the filesystem is unmounted.

We want to partition a disk or imagefile, using parted.

- Create a disk label (= partition table, thus making unusable any previous data. Remember that there's no commit, changes are immediate!)

- Set the default unit to MB

- Create three primary partitions

- Check the partition table

- Inform the OS of the partition table change

$ sudo parted /dev/loop1

# msdos for MBR; gpt for - well - GPT
(parted) mklabel msdos

(parted) unit MB

# 0% instead of 0 is a better choice for alignment
(parted) mkpart primary ext4 0% 256
(parted) mkpart primary ext4 256 512
(parted) mkpart primary ext4 512 1024

(parted) print

$ partprobe

We could then associate it with the first free loop device: $ losetup -Pf --show imagefile

Then, simply mount the single partitions, visible by $ ls -l /dev/loop1*

$ hddtemp

# in case of nvme

$ sudo apt-get install lm-sensors
$ sensors-detect
$ sensors
No fan speed doesn't mean there's no fan working... Yes, I know for a fact.

Ext4 filesystem are partitioned into disk block groups, that is sequences of bytes, and each contains:

Let's explain it.

Superblock: contains mount count (since last fsck) and maximum mount count; block size (4 KB); blocks per group; free block count; free inode count; operating system ID. 

Group Descriptors: contains the location of where the Data Block Bitmap, Inode Bitmap and Inode Table are in a Block Group.

Data Block Bitmap and Inode Bitmap are blocks whose bits contain 0 for each free block or inode and 1 for each used one. 

Inode Table: contains as many consecutive blocks as are necessary to cover the number of inodes in the block group. Each inode requires 128 bytes; thus, a 4 KB block can contain 32 inodes.

Linux Unified Key Setup (LUKS) is a specification for block device-level encryption (partitions and full/loop devices), recommended for portable systems such as laptops, tablets, and smart phones. LUKS is installed on top of the cryptsetup CLI, a frontend for the dm-crypt transparent disk encryption subsystem.

Let's create an encrypted partition, format is as EXT4 and make the system prompt for the passphrase and mount it on every reboot.

- Umount and remove the mapping

# make sure that our kernel has been compiled with encryption support
$ grep -i config_dm_crypt /boot/config-$(uname -r)
$ lsmod | grep dm_crypt

# set up LUKS partition (crypto-layer) and passphrase (will overwrite ALL data)
$ cryptsetup luksFormat /dev/sda7

# make the partition available by providing a passphrase (= create a mapping / un-encrypted passthrough device)
$ cryptsetup luksOpen /dev/sda7 secret-disk

# format as ext4
$ mkfs.ext4  /dev/mapper/secret-disk

# mount and umount
$ mount /dev/mapper/secret-disk /secret
$ umount /dev/mapper/secret-disk

# close LUKS
$ cryptsetup luksClose secret-disk

# /etc/fstab
/dev/mapper/secret-disk   /secret   ext4   defaults   1 2

# /etc/crypttab
secret-disk   /dev/sda7

You may have trouble here if your kernel does not support the default encryption method used by cryptsetup (dm-crypt). In that case, you can examine /proc/crypto to see the methods your system supports, and then you can supply a method, as in: $ cryptsetup luksFormat --cipher aes /dev/sda7
We have a coreutil basic command - like mv - which could take a long time. How to track progress?

# -m is for continous monitoring, and -w to extimate remaining time
$ progress -wm

This tool can be described as a Tiny, Dirty, Linux-Only C command that looks for coreutils basic commands (cp, mv, dd, tar, gzip/gunzip, cat, etc.) currently running on your system and displays the percentage of copied data.

Let's talk physical partitions or loopback files some more. Suppose we have used fdisk to create a new 250 MB partition on your system, probably on /dev/sda, and informed the OS of changes on the partition table. Now:

- Format a new filesystem on the partition just created, with a block size of choice.

- Make so the filesystem will be mounted at boot time and binary files may not be executed from it.

- Mount it as read-only.

# verbose way; the block size can be obmitted (one of 1024, 2048 and 4096)
$ mkfs.ext4 -b 4096 -v /dev/sda11

# /etc/fstab
# fs_freq: does it need to be dumped? Not used anymore!
# fs_passno: determine order of fsck @boot (root --> 1; other filesystems --> 2)
/dev/sda11 /mnt ext4 noexec[,loop] 0 2

# after /etc/fstab - now giving the mount dir is enough
$ mount -o ro[,loop] /mnt/tempdir
All the instances of /dev/sda11 would be replace with /imagefile if we were to use a loopback file.
We need to manage swap space.

### Examine your current swap space ###
# human readable and with partition; we could also $ cat /proc/swaps or  or $ htop
# swapon -s will also tell you about priorities, that is which comes first!
$ free -h 
$ swapon -s

### Add more swap space by adding a new swap file (2GB; a good minimum), but you could even use an actual partition ###
# /var/swapfile could be a good location: https://serverfault.com/questions/279248/linux-where-to-put-the-swap-file
# we could also use $ fallocate -l 2G /swapfile - see note below
$ sudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/var/swapfile bs=1M count=2048
$ sudo mkswap /var/swapfile
$ sudo swapon -v /var/swapfile
# disable?
$ sudo swapoff /var/swapfile

### Make it persistent ###
# will be something like: UUID=735b3be3-779c-4d21-a944-b033225f3ab4 none swap sw 0 0
$ echo "UUID=$(sudo blkid -s UUID -o value /swapfile) none swap sw 0 0" | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab

- Yes, if you're using a physical partition below /deb, it's just the same!

- You can keep a swap partition and add a swapfile too. The swap analysis commands will show the "grand total"
There does exist a tool for de-fragmenting ext4 filesystems...

# -c: don’t actually do anything, just analyze and report
$ e4defrag -c /var/log
Linux filesystems only tend to need defragmentation when they get very full, over 90 percent or so, or when they are small and have relatively large files, like when a boot partition is used.

Let's check & tune a filesystem. Dump an ext[2-4] filesystem information to a file and check for: 

   • The maximum mount count setting (after which a filesystem check will be forced).
 
   • The check interval (the amount of time after which a filesystem check is forced). 

   • The number of blocks reserved for privileged processes (to allow daemons, such as syslogd, to continue to function correctly, once non-privileged processes are prevented from writing to the filesystem), and the total number of blocks.

Then, change these values to 30, 3 weeks and 10%

# nice and tidy, compared to the gory $ dumpe2fs 
$ tune2fs -l /dev/sda5 | grep -i -E "mount count|check interval|block Count"

$ tune2fs -c 30 -i 3w -m 10 /dev/sda5
Doesn't work with mountpoints. Specify the partition location!

Let's encrypt the swap partition. That's important if you're using disk encryption, since the passphrase is saved there (from RAM) during hibernation!

- Format the encrypted device to use with swap and activate it 
- Ensure the encrypted swap partition can be activated and is mounted at boot

# set up a partition to be used for swap
$ cryptsetup luksFormat /dev/sda11
$ cryptsetup luksOpen /dev/sda11 swap

$ mkswap /dev/mapper/swapcrypt 
$ swapon /dev/mapper/swapcrypt

# verify
$ cryptsetup status swap
$ cat /proc/swaps

# /etc/fstab
/dev/mapper/swap   none   swap   defaults  0 0

# /etc/crypttab
swap /dev/sda11 /dev/urandom swap
Note: Using /dev/urandom as key means swap is re-encrypted with a new random key on every boot — data is not persistent across reboots. For persistent encrypted swap, use a passphrase or keyfile.

The structure of the Logical Volume Managment (LVM) - allowing to resize filesytems (except /) without rebooting - consists of:

- One or more entire hard disks or partitions configured as physical volumes (PVs)

- A volume group (VG) created using one or more PVs. You can think of a volume group as a single storage unit

- Multiple logical volumes (LG) that can then be created in a volume group. Each logical volume is somewhat equivalent to a traditional partition having its own filesystem - with the advantage that it can be resized.

- Grow the logical volume and corresponding filesystem

# Create PVs, list complete information and summary
$ pvcreate /dev/sdaX; pvcreate /dev/sdaY
$ pvscan && pvdisplay
$ pvs

# Create VGs, list complete information and summary
$ vgcreate vg00 /dev/sdaX /dev/sdaY 
$ vgscan && vgdisplay
$ vgs

# Create LVs, format, list complete information and summary
# percentage of the container? use: lvcreate -n vol_projects -l 100%FREE vg00
$ lvcreate -n vol_projects -L 10G vg00
$ lvscan && lvdisplay
$ lvs
$ mkfs.ext4 /dev/vg00/vol_projects

# we could also use a relative dimension, like --size +50M
$ lvresize -r --size 350M /dev/vg00/vol_projects

Suppose we have already created two partitions (physical or virtual). Let's:

- Create a RAID 1 device using the two partitions

- Add a spare (a partition used to replace a failing or failed disk)

- Format the RAID device as an ext4 filesystem and make the mount persistent

- Monitor the service, now and forevermore (also setting up a mail alert)

- Assemble the RAID

- Examine the status of your RAID device

# create
# the syntax loop[01] is equivalent to loop0 loop1
# real partitions? use /dev/sdaX /dev/sdaY
$ mdadm --create /dev/md0 --level=1 --raid-disks=2 /dev/loop[01]
$ mdadm --assemble --scan # Assemble the array

# spare
$ mdadm --add-spare /dev/loop02
$ mdadm /dev/md0 --add /dev/sdX1

# Ext4
$ mkfs.ext4 /dev/md0
cat "/dev/md0 /myraid ext4 defaults 0 0" >>  /etc/fstab

# set "MAILADDR root" in mdadm.conf, then:$ mdadm --detail --scan >> /etc/mdadm.conf
$ systemctl start mdmonitor && systemctl enable mdmonitor

# assemble
$ mdadm --assemble 

# much more information, compared to $ cat /proc/mdstat
$ mdadm --detail /dev/md0

- Convert to another compression (gz -> xz)
# create a compressed tar of all the files in a directory
# 'z' for .tgz compression, 'j' for .tbz2 and 'J' for .txz
# consider to cd into the directory before compression, to avoid having [dir] itself within the archive
$ tar zcvf [archive.ext] [dir]

# list files
$ tar tvf [archive]

# check of much space it shall occupy and then extract (no need to specify the compression!)
# the check of the final file works for tarred + compressed archives too...
# BUT only if the uncompressed size is < 4 Gib! Otherwise, random (negative) values
# this is a documented bug in $ man gunzip. In those cases, check with $ zcat file.gz | wc -c
$ gunzip -l [archive]
$ tar xvf [archive] [some-file-or-directory] -C [dir] --same-permissions

# add file
$ gunzip [archive.tar.gz]
$ tar rf [archive.tar] [file]
$ gzip -9 [archive.tar]

# extract/open a single file. But... disk-space-wise means to extract the whole archive :-(
$ vim [archive.tar.gz]

- No, no need for dashes! 

- We could update or trim down an archive, using $ tar df [archive.tar] [file] or $ tar uf [archive.tar] [file] but that won't work on compressed archives!

- We do it all with tar (decompress too) for the sake of our poor memory, but we could use specialized tools like $ gzip -d, $ bizp2 -d, $ xz -d

What is the difference between /tmp and /var/tmp?

/tmp is for temporary files not preserved between system reboots (Fedora and Ubuntu) or anyway removed by cron every 10 days (RHEL)

/var/tmp is for temporary files to be preserved between reboots.
What is the difference between device nodes (special device files) and sockets?

Device nodes: filesystem entries in /dev - managed by device drivers - which represent devices built into or connected to the system. They allow communication between program and devices by I/O system calls, like open(), read(), write() and close().

Sockets: client-server connections, necessary for network devices that transmit packets (constructed by breaking up streams, and later reconstructed into streams). When you close a socket connection, the socket is destroyed, and you must create a new socket before you can establish a new connection

I have a CSV file and I want to split in many files according to the values of a given column (sixth). We also want to:

- Skip the first line (field names)

- Remove some leading whitespace in the column of interest

# BTW, if you need to unite multiple CSV files, just...
$ cat [file1.csv] [file2.csv] > [union.csv]
$ awk -F ',' 'FNR > 1 { gsub(/^\s/,"",$6); print > ("split-" $6 ".csv") }' [file.csv]

- File separator (-F): comma

- Condition to check: skip the first line (FNR > 1)

- String substitution: gsub(...)

The column value is used to generate the resulting file name. So for example, every line with the value 42 will be in a file named split-42.csv

$ xmllint --format [YOUR_FILE] | highlight --syntax=xml --out-format=xterm256 | less -R -N
Or just install: https://github.com/fralau/xmlshow

We want a subset of lines from the CSV whose values in a column (fourth) matches values in the column (first) of another file. How?

# non-matching lines? add ! before k[$4]
$ awk -F, 'FNR==NR{k[$1]=1;next;} FNR==1 || k[$4]' [key.csv] [val.csv]

- First condition to check: (FNR==NR)
FNR is the number of lines read from current file and NR is the total number of lines read: if FNR==NR, we are still reading the first file. While reading, saves the value of KEY.csv (first column) in associative array k. Then, skips the rest of the defaults commands (print) and starts over on the next line.

- Second condition to check: (FNR==1 || k[$4])
True either for the first line of the file (field names), or for lines whose fourth field is in array k. If true true (or false, in the non-matching case), AWL performs the default action which is to print the line.

Ok, you wrote your shell script and you be thinking all is nice and safe and happy... Not! 

Here's a checklist.

# Shellcheck
$ shellcheck [script.sh]

# Debug mode - Place at the beginning of the script
set -n # syntax
set -v # echoes commands before execution
set -x # echos commands after execution
set -u # treat unset variables as errors

# Set current WorkDir as that of the script (if it's the case, of course!)
# Always check if "cd" was successful, or exit otherwise
cd "$(dirname "$0")" || exit
BTW: core dumped error? Are you sure you're not declaring a function with the same name of a built-in or installed command?

Here we find images greater than a certain size. To resize them, we could concatenate some more commands, but there's an easier way!

# syntax for the or operator: https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/50612/how-to-combine-2-name-conditions-in-find
$ find . \( -name "*.jpg" -o -name "*.png" \) -type f -exec identify -format '%w %h %i' '{}' \; | awk '$1<300 || $2<300'

# resize to 2000x the files greater than 2000
$ for file in *.png; do convert $file -resize 2000\> $file; done

# general sintax (percentage or specific size)
# if you don't care about AR, just add "!" after the width, for example 200x156!
# yes, a simple 200x156 will be ignored
$ convert -resize [50% | 200x] [original.png] [destination.png]

# wanna try up to the point when you reach a certain file size?
$ convert [input] -quality 10% [output] && ls -lh [output]

# multiple images, maintaining the filename and using only the width to preserve AR
$ convert "images/*.jpg[250x]" -set filename:base "%[base]" "images/new_folder/%[filename:base].jpg"

$ gifsicle [original.gif] --resize 250x250 > [resized.gif]

Suppose that a mv or a (s)cp of a directory into another filesystem (maybe resident in another server) failed halfway. We would need to start over! There's a much better alternative, and far far faster than any FTP shit...

# Tried and tested: SSH, archive mode (recursive, preserve timestamps & permissions), verbose, human-readable, stay on one filesystem, preserve ACLs and extended attributes (both not included in -a), show progress
$ rsync [-e "ssh -p non_22_port_number -i path_to_some_key"] --chown [user:group] -avhxAX --info=progress2 [source_1 ... source_n] [user@destination:/path/]

# When copying a file for the first time, avoid diff comparison with -W
# Compression (-z) is useless if working on a fast internet connession and could actually hinder performance because of CPU overhead. See card about it

* a trailing slash on the source means "copy the contents", while no trailing slash creates an additional directory level at the destination

* not sure what you're doing? --dry-run

* you can --remove-source-files (dangerous!) but there's no way to remove source dir(s)

* exclude something? This is relative to the source directory! Use --exclude '/foo' to exclude the 'foo' directory within the source. If you just use 'foo' ALL the 'foo' directories everywhere in the structrue to be copied will be skipped!

* remove extraneous objects in target? --delete

* --info=progress2 percentage is non very precise, but still more informative than a -vvv, to be used when debugging

This is how we show capacity and free space in RAM. And now you probably be thinking... "Linux ate my RAM" :-(

# human-readable is the staple
$ free -h

The "available" column (or the "-/+ buffers/cache" row) is the used and available memory, that applications could use with disk caching, that is before having to swap.

A healthy Linux system with more than enough memory will have:
- free memory close to 0
- used memory close to total 
- available memory (or "free + buffers/cache") has enough room (let's say, 20%+ of total) 
- swap used does not change

Warning signs:
- available memory (or "free + buffers/cache") is close to zero
- swap used increases or fluctuates
- dmesg | grep oom-killer shows the OutOfMemory-killer at work

This is how you can show in a human-readable way:

- The free space available

- The main consumers of memory (top-level dirs) - sorted by size

# Free Space: the grep is to exclude pseudo-filesystems
$ df -h | grep -E '^[^ ]*[/:]'

# Main Consumers: the -x is to exclude pseudo-filesystems
$ sudo du -cha --max-depth=1 -x / | sort -rh | grep -E "M|G"

# Main Consumers TUI: the -x is to exclude pseudo-filesystems
$ sudo ncdu -rx /

# So full that you cannot even use ncdu?
# Check link at https://dev.yorhel.nl/ncdu
$ curl [static_binary_link]  --output ncdu.tar.gz
$ tar xvzf *.tar.gz
$ ./ncdu -rx /

"pseudo-filesystem" means a filesystem that doesn't have actual files – rather, it has virtual entries that the filesystem itself makes up on the spot: /proc dynamically generates directories for every process; /sys files and directories to represent hardware layouts.

The Windows equivalent is WinDirStat
We want some persistent security hardening on Linux kernel parameters. How?

# show all kernel parameters
$ sysctl -a

# test a single value
$ sysctl net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_all=1

# /etc/sysctl.conf

# Turn on execshield
kernel.exec-shield=1
kernel.randomize_va_space=1

# Enable IP spoofing protection
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter=1

# Disable IP source routing
net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route=0

# Ignoring broadcasts request
net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts=1
net.ipv4.icmp_ignore_bogus_error_messages=1

# Make sure spoofed packets get logged
net.ipv4.conf.all.log_martians = 1
net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_all=1

# you won't even have to reboot!
$ sysctl -p
Processes have a niceness, but a badness too. What is it?
In order to make decisions of who gets sacrificed to keep the system alive by the OOM KIller, a value called the badness is computed, read from /proc/[pid]/oom_score.
