Let's do extended attributes.

- Try to add the append-only attribute to a file. 

- Set the immutable attribute to it.

- Take a look at the actual extended attributes of the file.

- Remove 'em.

# you can ONLY append (>>) not overwrite (>) to the file
$ chattr +a [file]

# can't be moved, deleted, edited, linked by hard links... not even by root!
$ chattr +i [file]

$ lsattr [file]

$ chattr -ia appendit

For information:

d: no-dump - Ignored when the dump program is run. This is useful for swap and cache files that you don't want to waste time backing up.

A: no atime update - Will not modify its atime (access time) record when the file is accessed but not otherwise modified. This can increase the performance on some systems because it reduces the amount of disk I/O on the system.

Insecure permissions when swapping on. 
swapon: /tmp/swpfile: insecure permissions 0664, 0600 suggested.

We can verify it using the commands to show file permissions. What about chaing it, in both the octal and symbolic notation?

# octal notation: -c == --format; %a = octal note; %n = filename
$ stat -c "%a %n" /tmp/swpfile

# symbolic notation
$ ls -l /tmp/swpfile

$ chmod u-w,g-w,o-r /tmp/swpfile

$ chmod 600 /tmp/swpfile

- The presence of a certain permission equals a power of 2 (r=2^2, w=2^1, x=2^0). In case of directories, "read" means "shown contents", "write" means "edit contents", "execute" means "traverse, that is cd into the directory"

- When using the symbolic notation, if you need to edit a permission for all (u,g,o) just use something like "a+x"

- BTW... If a group has write permission on the file, an user of that group can chown :other_group, but cannot change the owner

- Here we are talking about standard Unix DAS Access Control, but per-user and per-group ACLs (setfacl) have higher priority!
Let's us dd for backing up a whole disk, and then restore it. Optional: what about monitoring progress?

$ dd if=/dev/sda [status=progess] | gzip -c > /system_images/sda.img.gz 

$ gzip -dc /system_images/sda.img.gz | dd of=/dev/sda

# in case you don't have status=progress, sending a USR1 signal to a running process makes it print I/O statistics to standard error
$ watch -n 10 "pkill -usr1 dd"

Let's talk mount errors, when mounting an image file.

wrong fs type, bad option, bad superblock on /dev/loop0, missing codepage or helper program, or other error

- For some reason, the file might be corrupted. If it your own, delete it and create anew!

- Has it actually been formatted by mkfs?!

- Are you trying to mount a whole disk, having multple partitions?

You can get the current PIDs being issued by using $ ls & (or any other background process), but that number (and thus the number of processes you can have on the system) is not endless: when the PID reaches the value of kernel tunable pid_max, conventionally 32768 (32K), it will wrap around to lower numbers. 

- Find out what current PIDs are being issued, when starting a new process.

- Reset pid_max to a lower value than the ones currently being issued. 

- Make the change persistent.

$ cat /proc/sys/kernel/pid_max

# the shell does output redirection, not sudo or echo, so this would be done as a regular user even when using sudo
# thus, you need to be root!
# echo 24000 > /proc/sys/kernel/pid_max

# /etc/sysctl.conf
kernel.pid_max = 24000
When talking about device files, what is the difference between major and minor numbers?

They identify the driver associated to a device node.

major (8): device nodes of the same type (character or block) with the same major, use the same driver.

minor (0,1,2): used by the device driver to differentiate between the different devices it may control.
What is the difference between a kernal panic and a kernel oops?

An oops is a specific error, contaning:

- Brief description
- Oops #
- Which CPU it happened on, and the code the CPU was executing
- Register contents

With an oops, the system can usually continue. A panic means the system cannot recover and must be restarted.

udev (User Device) is a kernel facility - replacing the older devfs and hotplug - which is responsible for figuring out which devices are present, locating the drivers they need to operate properly, and loading them as entries under /dev (dynamical generation of device nodes). It also allows persistent device node naming: names do not depend on the order of device connection or plugging in! Now:
 
1. Create and implement a rule that will create a symlink called myusb when a USB device is plugged in.

2. On a separate terminal, continuously monitor the kernel ring buffer.

3. Plug in a USB device to your system and see if your symlink was created.

# /etc/udev/rules.d/my-usb.rules
SUBSYSTEM=="usb", SYMLINK+="myusb"

$ dmesg -wT

$ ls -lF /dev | grep myusb
What's the main difference between KVM and QEMU?

KVM (Kernel-based virtual machine) is kernel hypervisor: a virtualization infrastructure, requiring specialized hardware. Fast performance!

QEMU is an emulator, not requiring specialized hardware. Used for developing an operating system for a new CPU before hardware is available, or running virtual machines on different architectures, such a pretend ARM guest machine on an X86 host. Slow performance!

Best solution: use 'em together! KVM arbitrates access to the CPU and memory, and QEMU emulates the hardware resources (hard disk, video, USB, etc.).

Let's make sure KVM is properly set up and create (=install) a virtual machine

- Check that you have hardware virtualization available and enabled (you may need to turn it on from the BIOS settings)

- Start the necessary service

- Install a machine:

RAM: 512 MB (Note that the host must have at least 1024 MB)
1 virtual CPU
8 GB disk
Name: centos7vm

# Where vmx is for INTEL CPUs and svm for AMD
$ grep --color -E 'vmx|svm' /proc/cpuinfo

$ systemctl start libvirtd

# look up "--os-variant" online
# see note about --cpu
$ virt-install --name=centos7vm --ram=1024 --vcpus=1 --cdrom=/var/lib/libvirt/images/CentOS-7-x86_64-Minimal-1804.iso --os-type=linux --os-variant=rhel7 --cpu=host --network type=direct,source=eth0 --disk path=/var/lib/libvirt/images/centos7vm.dsk,size=8

# wanna see the GUI?
$ virt-viewer [domain-name]

- If you do not have hardware virtualization, you may be able to run virt-manager, but with weak performance.

- About --cpu, for homogeneous clusters and single host setups use the host option. For mixed clusters, use the lowest available CPU version, so if one host is Penryn and the other Nehalem, use Penryn on both. 

- Do not run libvirtd at the same time as another hypervisor as dire consequences are likely to arise! systemctl stop vmware, vboxdrv etc

- If runing virt-install as root, it connects to qemu:///system and by default libvit will run QEMU as a qemu:qemu user/group pair. Home directories on most distros prevent any other user from seeing files beneath them. The best thing is to just move the ISO into /var/lib/libvirt/images if you want to launch VMs as root. If you don't have it, you're probably missing some packages...

- The --network "source" is your actual ethernet/wi-fi card! Check it
What is a sparse file?

A type of computer file that attempts to use file system space more efficiently when the file itself is partially empty. This is achieved by writing brief information (metadata) representing the empty blocks to disk instead of the actual "empty" space which makes up the block, using less disk space.

$ docker rm [container-ID] # $ docker rm $(docker ps -a -q)

$ passwd -u [user]

Let's ssh from the client (/ˈklaɪənt/) side for key-based authentication

- Generate a private (id_rsa) and a public (id_rsa.pub) encryption key for a user

- Cache the private key, so you won't be asked for passphrase

- Copy public key to remote host, then identify with login/pass for the last time. What if we already have access to that machine?

$ ssh-keygen

$ eval $(ssh-agent)
$ ssh-add $HOME/.ssh/id_rsa

# NO ACCESS
$ ssh-copy-id [remote_host]

# ACCESS
$ sudo cat id_rsa.pub >> /remote/home/my_user/.ssh/authorized_keys

Let's reset the root mariadb password.

# stop server
$ systemctl stop mariadb
$ pkill -c mysql

# start without user privilege information and (safety-wise) no network
$ sudo -u mysql:mysql mkdir /var/run/mysqld
$ mysqld_safe --skip-grant-tables --skip-networking &

# no questions asked!
$ mysql -u root

# If you're using MariaDB, you need to load the Unix Socket Authentication Plugin on the server side.
# /etc/mysql/my.cnf
# don't forget the group, that is [mysqld]
[mysqld]
plugin-load-add = auth_socket.so

MariaDB [(none)]> FLUSH PRIVILEGES; # necessary, since we used --skip-grant-tables
MariaDB [(none)]> ALTER USER 'root'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'new_password';

# stop, start, login
$ systemctl stop mariadb && systemctl start mariadb
The systemctl service, instead of "mariadb", could be (even if it's using Maria...) just "mysql" (yes, without the "d")..
What's the difference between relative, absolute and canonical pathnames?

Absolute: a pathname beginning with at least one one <slash> characters. Can contain /./ or /../

Relative: a pathname not beginning with a <slash> character. Can contain /./ or /../

Canonical: a resolved pathname. Can NOT contain /./ or /../
What is the main difference - and thus how can I choose - between sed, awk and tr?

sed --> line-oriented

awk --> field-oriented

tr --> character-oriented

Let's talk user accounts.

- Create an account which will use the Korn shell (ksh) as its default and make sure it has a home directory

- Set his password and show its status, but... make it so he will have to change the password on first login

- Change the home, the shell and the account expiration date

- Check UID, GID and groups for this user

- Delete his account, including home and mail's spool

$ useradd -ms /bin/ksh [user]

# default password (in /etc/passwd): !! or ! thus you have to set it
$ passwd --expire [user]
$ passwd -S [user]

$ usermod -e [date] -30 -d /tmp -s /bin/sh [user]

# we could use groups [user], but id provides more information
$ id [user]

# -r is to also delete the user home
$ userdel -r [user]
When using passwd -S, you'll see if the user account has a locked password (L or LK), has no password (NP), or has a usable password (P or PS).

1. Start a restricted shell in your current window.

2. Set up a restricted account.

$ bash -r

# symlink named rbash pointing to bash? Invokes bash directly, but bash recognizes it was invoked through rbash and comes up as a restricted shell
$ ln /bin/bash /bin/rbash
$ useradd -s /bin/rbash fool
$ passwd fool

/etc/password and /etc/group have 644 permission. Anyone can read (you would just need Crack or John The Ripper!) while /etc/shadow and /etc/gshadow have 400 permission: only root can read. We can edit these files using specific programs settings locks, to avoid file corruption. But this still doesn't mean we have secure passwords! What about it?

# -s it to open the 'shadow' version
$ vipw [-s]
$ vigr [-s]

# we could specify the salt with -S, but a random one is provided by default; -R is the number of interations
$ mkpasswd [user_name] -m sha-512 -R 5000000

Wil generate a line to be copied in the shadow files: we have an identifier for the hash function ($6 is sha-512), $rounds, the $salt and finally the hash.
$6$rounds=5000000$Lc5mF7Mm$FWm/GeTLTryHa0Nt/WfrbLqjVOsipSBNP3IUgwbNP7H95eR8 lhKj.6Pc7YcznupXjHXA9QBirkmmaxh3oqt4v
What is the difference between primary, secondary and user private groups?

Primary --> One for each user (created by default). GID used whenever the user creates files/directories.  When creating an user by useradd, UID > UID_MIN (defined in /etc/login.defs), group_name = user_name (User Private Group) and GID = UID.

Secondary --> Up to 15 for each user. Additional permissions.
The default permissions given when creating a file are read/write for owner, group and world (0666), and for a directory it is read/write/execute for everyone (0777). However...

As soon as they are created, you will notice the actual permissions have changed to 664 for the file and 775 for the directory. They have been modified by the current $ umask (0002) whose purpose is to show which permissions should be denied: 0666 AND ~002 = 0664

Linux contains a full implementation of  POSIX ACLs (Access Control Lists) which extends the simpler u-g-o and r-w-x models. Particular privileges can be granted to specific users or groups of users when accessing certain objects or classes of objects. Now:

- See the ACLs properties for a file (btw, more readable than a $ ls -ld [dir] )

-  Make the file readable and executable by user 'fool', then remove this permission

- Remove all ACLs from a file

# get File ACL
$ getfacl [file]

# --modify
$ setfacl -m u:fool:rx [file]
$ setfacl -x u:fool [file]

$ setfacl -b [file]

ACLs can be recognized with a "+" at the end of permissions string when using ls -l. In that case, the only things you can do to have precise information is to use getfacl and thus see the effective rights mask.

Remember that Unix ACLs are not supported on NFS!

Today, we do quotas so that: create bigfile1 (200 blocks --> ok), bigfile2 (400 blocks --> warning soft limit), bigfile3 (600 blocks --> error hard limit). Why?

- Change the entry in /etc/fstab for your filesystems to use user and group quotas.

- Create the quota files and initialize user/group quotas on the filesystem

- Now set some quota limits for a user account: a soft limit of 500 blocks and a hard limit of 1000 blocks.

- Turn the quota off

- Show current quotas

# /etc/fstab
/imagefile /mount_dir ext4 loop,usrquota,grpquota 1 2

# create /quota-fs/aquota.user and aquota.group, without read-only remount (= on active system) 
$ quotacheck -cugm /quota-fs
$ quotaon -ug /mnt/quota-fs

$ edquota -u [student]

$ quotaoff -u /mnt/quota-fs

Users who violate a soft limit quota get an e-mail indicating that they have a few days' grace period before the penalties kick in. Users who cross the hard limit threshold get no such grace period. The penalty differs depending on the particular quota, but usually nearly every command will fail such that the user will want to come into compliance quickly.

auth                    required    pam_tally2.so                                    deny=3     onerr=fail 
account              required    pam_tally2.so

$ pam_tally2 -u [user]
$ pam_tally2 -u [user] -r

- The format is:

module-type      control     module-path (in lib64/security)     module-arguments


- The last two hashed passwords of each user are saved in /etc/security/opasswd so that they cannot be reused

- No need to remember! Just consult the examples with $ man pam_tally2

Let's match:

- any of multiple patterns (grep OR) - comments or empty lines

- negative pattern (grep NOT) - not a comment and not an empty line

$ firewall-cmd --zone=trusted --remove-interface=eno1

The swappiness parameter controls the tendency of the kernel to move processes out of physical memory and onto the swap disk. The default setting in Ubuntu or Fedora is swappiness=60, suitable for servers. For desktop systems, a value of swappiness=10 is recommended. How to check it and change it, both on a temporary and a permanent base?

# change on the fly
$ bash -c "echo 10 > /proc/sys/vm/swappiness"

# /etc/sysctl.conf
$ bash -c "echo 'vm.swappiness = 15' >> /etc/sysctl.conf"

Note: The bash -c "echo ... > /proc/sys/vm/..." form requires running as root. If using sudo, prefer: echo 10 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/swappiness (redirections in sudo bash -c work, but tee is cleaner).

We want some way to scan log files like /var/log/auth.log and ban IP addresses having too many failed login attempts (by updating system firewall rules). The answer is https://github.com/fail2ban/fail2ban 
Suppose we locked ourselves out. 

- Get a list of active jails (that is, having enabled = true propriety)

- Get a list of banned IPs for a jail

- Get a list of whitelisted IP for a jail

- Unban an IP

- Whitelist an IP  (for all jails)

$ fail2ban-client status

# to check if there are connections at all from a specific address, use $ netstat -na | grep "address"
$ sudo iptables -L -n | awk '$1=="REJECT" && $4!="0.0.0.0/0"'
$ sudo zgrep 'Ban' /var/log/fail2ban.log*

$ fail2ban-client set [jail-name] unbanip [ip-address]

$ fail2ban-client get [jail-name] ignoreip

# /etc/fail2ban/jail.local
[DEFAULT]
ignoreip = 127.0.0.1/8 88.216.145.201 [etc]

# restart! reload is not applicable for this kind of unit
$ systemctl restart fail2ban

# if you use a split VPN, the outbound address could be different for another endpoint!
$ curl ifconfig.me

# Route-specific solution: check the "src" field
$ ip route get "$(dig +short some-address.com)"

To implement mandatory access controls over requests made to the kernel, we can choose one (and only one) among a few LSMs (Linux Security Modules): SELinux, AppArmor, Smack, TOMOYO. The basic idea is to hook system calls; that means...
insert security-related code whenever an application requests a transition to kernel (system) mode in order to accomplish work that requires enhanced abilities.

Security-Enhanced Linux (SELinux) is a module (/ˈmɑːdʒuːl/) providing access control can be run under one of the three following 

- Modes: enforcing, permissive (only prints warnings) and disabled

- Policies: targeted (selected processes + memory restrictions for all processes --> reduced vulnerability to buffer overflow attacks), minimum (only selected processes), MLS (Multi-Level Security: fine-grained security domains with particular policies for all processes)

- Contexts: labels applied to files, directories, ports, and processes to describe access rules - User, Role, Type, Level. 

How can we activate SELinux, check its status, change the mode/policy and check contexts for processes and files?

# edit /etc/selinux/config + reboot for activation

$ sestatus

# check/set contexts: usually -Z option in common commands like cp, mv, ps, mkdir
$ ps -axZ
$ ls -Z
To change from Permissive to Enforcing or viceversa, we could also use $ sudo setenforce Permissive | Enforcing

Let's tackle some classical SELinux issues.

- Configure the default context for a newly created directory

- Since the web server can only access files with the correct context labels, creating a file in /tmp, and then moving it to the DocumentRoot directory / or to another directory, will make the file unaccessible to the httpd server until the SELinux context of the file is adjusted.

# Add context (-a), from type (-t) default_t to httpd_sys_content_t for the /virtualHosts dir
$ semanage fcontext -at httpd_sys_content_t /virtualHosts

# reset file contexts, based on parent directory settings.
$ restorecon -Rv /virtualHosts

# check it
$ ls -Z
semanage fcontext is provided by the policycoreutils-python package
How can you get your WI-FI (wireless) password?

# Fedora
$ cat /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/keys-{SSID}

# Ubuntu
$ sudo grep -r '^psk=' /etc/NetworkManager/system-connections/
Rename multiple files (or directories) at once, changing a pattern? Suppose we want to remove "DILMUN_"

# -n is for dry-running
$ rename -n 's/abc/xyz/' *

We want to list:

- Phyiscal drives

- Physical storage partitions

We could use any of these. But we want a well-formatted ouput: human-redable size, hierachical (thus showing disks, encrypted volumes and LVMs at a glance), with filesystem types and permissions...

$ cat /proc/partitions

$ sudo fdisk -l

$ sudo parted -l

$ sudo blkid

# all the entries in the left will have their equivalent /dev device, as you would see usng the methods above
# double check which are the physical ones? $ lsblk -d
$ lsblk -fm
Moreover, it doesn't require being root
This is a cromulent way how to resize a partition (and its EXT filesystem), possibly without rebooting.

# OLD WAY
# change partition table
# delete (d) the partition (or more than one, if space is needed)
# create a new one (n) EXACTLY ad the same START point, with same Id (83 for Linux)
# restore the boot flag (a) and write (w)
$ fdisk [disk]
$ partprobe -s

# MODERN WAY
# partition on /dev/sda, like /dev/sdaX
# in Debian, you need BOTH cloud-guest-utils and fdisk
$ sudo growpart /dev/[partition_name] [partition_number]
$ resize2fs /dev/[partition_name]/[partition_number]
When there's a diference in the % of available space between df -h and lsblk -fm, it means a partion has been resized but the file system has not be enlarged accordingly

Network Address Translation (NAT) allows for multiple network hosts to share the same external IP address. Enabling NAT (any type) is necessary if you have more than one network interface when using IPv4, before you can use iptables/firewalld, and that means you have to turn on ip forwarding: forward network traffic from one interface to another. 

- Check current value of the relative kernel runtime parameter (/proc/sys files)

- Change it

$ sysctl -a | grep ip_forward

# temporary - two options 
$ echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward
$ sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

# persistent - one option (edit /etc/sysctl.conf)
net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
$ sudo sysctl -p

The traditional System V Runlevels (SysV Init) defined what tasks could be accomplished in the current execution state (runlevel), from 0 to 6.

Here we see how to see the current runlevel and how to switch to another one. what about enabling services for a specific runlevel?

$ runlevel

# beware! this won't inform the users
$ [tel]init [runlevel]

$ chkconfig --list [service name]

# or service off
$ chkconfig --level [level(s)] service on

This is CentOs stuff. In Debian, we could use a very simple TUI like $ sysv-rc-conf

id:[runlevel]:initdefault:

After you have created a guest domain (either a running virtual machine or a configuration which can be used to launch one), here are some commands you can use to manage:

- Edit the settings of centos7vm in your default text editor

- Enable or disable autostart to have the virtual machine boot (or not) when the host does

- Change the allocated RAM to 16GB

- Change the allocated disk space (thus, also stop and start the machine) of +10GB for one (there can be many!) of the disks

- Start the machine and check

- Delete it

# very important to use --all, or inactive domains (shutdown machines) won't be visible!
$ virsh list --all

# info about a specific VM
$ virsh dominfo [vm_name]

# changing name? you might need another uuid, to be generated with $ uuidgen
$ virsh edit [vm_name]

$ virsh [autostart | --disable] [vm_name]

# first you may need to $ virsh setmaxmem [vm name] 16G --config
$ virsh setmem [vm_name] 16GB --config

# "shutdown" is equivalent to "destroy --graceful"; use $ virsh destroy [vm_name] if you want it ungraceful
$ virsh shutdown [vm_name]

# increase
$ qemu-img resize /var/lib/libvirt/images/[something].qcow2 +10G

# shrinking - way more comples
# see here: https://libguestfs.org/virt-resize.1.html

# then SSH in the VM and fdisk + resize2fs or the like (see related card)
$ virsh start [vm_name] 

# bear in mind that the file system size of the cow2 file (or img) will be the same before and after the resize...
# ... since that's the actually occupied data, not the virtual one
# to understand: is there a way to see it, at least when the VM is shut down? try with: sudo qemu-img info /var/lib/libvirt/images/[something].qcow2

# this only works after shutting down
$ virsh undefine [vm_name]

How can I check whether a TCP/UDP port on a server is open (= a daemon process is listening on it, thus implying the service must be active)? More generally, we could say we are checking active Internet (TCP and UDP) connections.

Check both from localhost and from remote.

# FROM LOCALHOST, INBOUND CONNECTIONS
#--tcp --udp --program (PID) --listening (only) --extend (verbose) --numeric (port)
# roughly equivalent to Windows nestat -ano, but in Windows you'll likely want to use TCPView
# netstat is part of the the net-tools package
$ netstat -tuplen | grep [:port]
$ telnet localhost [port] 

# FROM LOCALHOST, OUTBOUND CONNECTIONS
# Use portquiz.net - https://medium.com/@suixo/how-to-scan-your-internet-link-for-restricted-outgoing-open-ports-396fd0503cfb
$ nmap -p [port] portquiz.net

# FROM Linux REMOTE
# All the alternatives :-) 
# nmap by default works with TCP, to use UDP go with -sU -p [port]
$ nmap --reason --resolve-all -Pn -p [port] [destination-IP | hostname]  
$ nc -v [destination-IP | hostname] [port] # no output? closed.
$ telnet [destination-IP] [port] 
$ echo > /dev/tcp/[destination-IP]/5432

# FROM PS REMOTE
PS> Test-NetConnection -ComputerName [IP] -Port [Port]

"filtered" means the packet is dropped (no reply whatsoever), while "closed" means the packet is rejected. Basically, filtered means something in your machine, ISP, router, nework is filtering the port!

To close telnet, use Control + ] and then ctrl+D. Yes...
How can we password-protect a sub-directory? Let's say that only "sammy" and "bob" can access the "secure" path or a specific directory.

# /etc/apache2/sites-enabled/secure-dir.conf || /etc/httpd/conf.d/secure-dir.conf | 
<Location "/secure/">
   AuthType Basic
   AuthName "Restricted Area"
   AuthUserFile /etc/apache2/.htpasswd
   Require valid-user
</Location>

# or...
<Directory "/var/www/html">
        AuthType Basic
        AuthName "Restricted Area"
        AuthUserFile /etc/apache2/.htpasswd
        Require valid-user
</Directory>

# The same syntax can be used within an .htaccess file, but in that case without Directory and without Location
# The path must be absolute! https://stackoverflow.com/questions/6111627/how-to-use-a-relative-path-with-authuserfile-in-htaccess
# Yes, you need a file and cannot just write in user/pass

# additional users? same command, but just leave out the '-c' argument
$ sudo htpasswd -c [auth_user_file] [sammy]

$ systemctl restart [httpd | apache2]

# check
$ curl -u "[user:pass]" [address]

- Keep in mind that password protection should be combined with SSL encryption so that your credentials are not sent to the server in plain text.

- Bear in mind that htpasswd uses a bcrypt / MD5 algorithm with an hash

Environment variables can be command-wise, local (session-wide), but also user-wide and system-wide. This is how we set or unset 'em. Now, what about doing substitutions in a file chock full of such variables?

# command/script-wise (useful in loops and the like; since these are not available to shell sub-processes)
NAME='Value'

# session-wide
$ export NAME='Value'
$ unset NAME

export NAME='Value'
$ source [file]

# system-wide: add or remove to /etc/bash.bashrc (local logins) or /etc/profile (remote SSH logins) or /etc/environment for both
export NAME='Value'
$ source [file]

# suppose source.txt is full of shit like $PROJECT_ID and the like, that we have previously exported

BIND (Berkeley Internet Name Domain) alias named is the de facto standard DNS service / server daemon. Now, we want to configure a caching DNS server.

- Change to configuration to listen on a public interface (= port and IP), instead of loopback

- Check your configuration

- Activate it

- Keep an eye on it...

# /etc/bind/named.conf or /etc/named.conf
options {
   listen-on port 53 { any; };
   allow-query { any; };
   # Debian/Ubuntu only
   recursion yes;
}

# "Has no NS records" or the like probably means some identation errors
#`IN NS localhost.; should have a tab before it
$ sudo named-checkconf

$ sudo systemctl restart [named | bind9]

$ sudo journalctl -u [named | bind9] -f
For a client (ˈklaɪənt/) to actually use this server, remember to add 'nameserver server-IP' to the top of its /etc/resolv.conf

Let's talk special permissions.

SUID (set user ID): enables other users to run the executable file with permissions of the owner: "s" instead of "x" in the user octal. Very dangeous! To be used with privilege drop: drop the root privileges (in the executed script) as soon ad their goal is finished.
Example: /usr/bin/passwd, accessing the usally for-root-hands-only /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow; /bin/ping

SGID (set group ID): enables other users to inherit the GID of file group owner: "s" instead of "x" in the group octal.
Example: a collaborative directory. When I create a file or sub-dir, I will be the owner, but the group will be the same of the sgid parent directory.

Sticky Bit (alias "restricted deletion bit"): makes a directory such that only the file's owner, the directory's owner, or root user can rename or delete the file within it: "t" instead of "x" in the other octal. 
Example: /tmp directory. Everyone can create file, but I cannot delete files created by other people.

Now, let's set SUID or SGID for a file; sticky bit for a directory and then find files having the SUID, SGID or both set in the current directory.

$ chmod u+s [file] # chmod 4xxx

$ chmod g+s [file] # chmod 2xxx

$ chmod +t [dir] # chmod 1xxx

$ find . -perm /[value] # /1000 for sticky, /4000 for suid, /2000 for gsid, /6000 for both

This is probably one of my most irksome things that people mess up all the time
https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/79401/92223

Beware of doing it on executable files! See related card

Iptables is a kernel module (/ˈmɑːdʒuːl/) (if needed, just use $ modprobe ip_tables) serving as frontend to the netfilter framework, ultimate responsible for implementing a firewall to perform packet filtering, packet mangling (transformation) and NAT functionalities or redirection. Now, let's:

- View the rules for all the chains (or a single chain), and the default policy for each

- Flush (delete) all temporary rules from the INPUT chain to make sure our packets will be tested with new rules

- Change the default policy for the INPUT chain to DROP (== no more connections of any kind!)

- Make these changes persitent

# if there are "pkts" and "bytes" it means there are packets matching the rules 
$ iptables -v -L [CHAIN-NAME]

$ iptables -F INPUT

$ iptables -P INPUT DROP

# if really needed, disable/mask firewalld, install iptables-services and systemctl start iptables 
$ service iptables save

Options for ports are to be found in $ man iptables-extensions not $ man iptables !!!

- Historical note: iptables replaces the older Linux IP Firewalling Chains (ipchains).

- Rules are inspected sequentially: later rules could never be matched, if the packets are accepted by the previous ones

You knew this day would come. Explain the differences between grep -E (alias the deprecated egrep), grep -F (alias the deprecated fgrep) and grep -P.

grep --> Basic Regular Expressions (BRE): meta-characters like: '{','}','(',')','|','+','?' loose their meaning and are treated as normal characters of string and need to be escaped if they are to be treated as special characters.

grep -E --> Extended Regular Expression (ERE): meta-characters are treated as special characters and you are freed from the burden of escaping them!

grep -F --> for searching any direct string, this is the version of grep which should be selected! Doesn’t even recognize special characters as part of regular expression, escaped or not!

grep -P --> Perl Compatible Regular Expression (PCRE): like in PHP!

Let's do some AWK basics. We have a CSV source file and we want to:

- Print only some columns (by their number), one of them with quotes around it, and add a new column

- Use a custom separator (input and output)

# FS is the source field separator; OFS the output field separator

# Check if any argument was provided

fi

# Check if a specific argument was provided
if [[ -z "$4" ]]; then
    echo "Fourth argument not supplied"

fi

### PROFTPD ###

# /etc/proftpd/proftpd.conf
AuthUserFile /etc/proftpd/ftpd.passwd
RequireValidShell Off
DefaultRoot  ~ # very important! chroot jail, so users won't roam freely!

# Create virtual FTP users ("virtual" means they are not in /etc/passwd), change a password, remove it
# UID and GUID are those of a Linux user with permissions fot the target directory
# in this case we are using a non-standard file location!
$ ftpasswd --passwd --uid=[system_user_uid] --gid=[system_user_gid] --home=[some_path] --shell=/bin/false --name=[user] --file=/etc/proftpd/ftpd.passwd
$ ftpasswd --passwd --change-password --name=[user] --file=/etc/proftpd/ftpd.passwd
$ ftpasswd --passwd --delete-user --name=[user] --file=/etc/proftpd/ftpd.passwd

# List all users
$ cat /etc/proftpd/ftpd.passwd

# List users online
$ ftpwho

### PUREFTPD ###

# Add virtual user
$ pure-pw useradd [user] -u [system_username] -d [some_path] -m
$ pure-pw mkdb

Execute a query (/ˈkwɪri/) (search for a string) on all the databases.

#!/bin/bash

# mysql credential 
user="root"
pass="o46lpDsHQr0_Svk0zgmAD-bDVD8sPM0w"

# list of all databases
all_dbs="$(mysql -u $user -p$pass -Bse 'show databases')"        

# cycle
for db in $all_dbs
     do
        if test "$db" != "information_schema" 
            then if test "$db" != "mysql" 
                 then
                    mysql -u$user -p$pass "$db" -sN -e "SELECT DATABASE(); ..."
                    fi
        fi  
     done
Security note: Never hardcode credentials in scripts. Use ~/.my.cnf (mode 600) with [client] section, or mysql_config_editor, instead of plaintext user/pass variables.

Enter OfflineIMAP: 

- Downloads mailbox(es) as local Maildirs, meaning reading your email offline - including attachments - without the need for your mail reader (MUA) to support IMAP operations
- Have your own backups, if you don't trust your provider
- Migrate from one hosting service to another!

How, sweet Jesus?

# ~/.offlineimaprc - Default, but it can be anywhere
[general] # List of accounts to be synced, separated by a comma.
accounts = main

[Account main] # Identifier for the local repository; e.g. the maildir to be synced via IMAP.
localrepository = main-local
remoterepository = main-remote # Identifier for the remote repository; i.e. the actual IMAP, usually non-local. 
[Repository main-local]
type = Maildir # OfflineIMAP supports Maildir, GmailMaildir, and IMAP for local repositories.
localfolders = ~/mail # Where should the mail be placed? 
[Repository main-remote] # "Remote" repos can be IMAP or Gmail, the latter being a preconfigured IMAP. "Remote" can be on this server, too!
type = IMAP
remotehost = host.domain.tld
remoteuser = username
readonly = true # don't do anything over there!
createfolders = false # by default, it creates directory in remote too!
# need to debug errors? Use offlineimap -dALL
$ offlineimap [-c some_configuration_file] -l offlineimap.log # execute it, boy!
What is a bridge (alias repeater)?

A [virtual or physical] network device accepting packets on one side and passing them through (bidirectional):

    - It is a combination of two or more networks at Layer 2.

    - Bridged networks communicate as a single network.


- The virtual interfaces are commonly used in virtualization (e.g., with KVM, Docker, or LXC) to connect VMs or containers to the host network. Check with ip link show type bridge

A DNS answer is "authoritative" if it comes from authoritative nameservers (there can be many!), meaning with a DNS zone configured for a certain domain, and a non-authoritative answer comes from recursive nameservers (like your router or your ISP). 

Now, we want to:

- Have a list of authoritative nameservers for a domain

- Check if a dig answer from a specific nameserver is authoritative

- Show the whole recursion, for geek's fun

- Since authoritative does not mean "configured at the registrar level"...

$ dig +short NS [domain]

# Look for the flags on the third line... Is there an "aa"? Then it's authoritative
# BTW, TTL is stable on aa, while it decreses when querying non authoritative servers
$ dig @[ns] [domain]

$ dig +trace [domain]

# classic!
$ whois [domain]
The only way to have an authoritative answer is it to know and esplicitly query (/ˈkwɪri/) an authoritative server.

$ journalctl -u [service] -f 

$ systemctl stop [service]

# command not found? apk add openrc
$ rc-service [service] start
$ rc-service --list | grep [service]
$ rc-service [service] stop

# All manual! We use "vsftpd" as an example
$ /usr/sbin/vsftpd /etc/vsftpd/vsftpd.conf &
$ ps -ef | grep vsftpd
$ killall vsftpd

The contents of the stress package does not include a systemd unit configuration so one must be created. Let's:

Description=Example service unit to run stress
[Service]

Let's use TCP Wrappers (a host-based networking ACL system, used to filter access to Internet Protocol servers/daemons) to disable access to the vsftpd service, assuming we have already started it. Remember to check (before and after) and to clean up at the end!

- TCP Wrappers option is disabled by default in Ubuntu and Debian. You must add the following line to the end of /etc/vsftpd.conf:
tcp_wrappers=yes

# check before
$ telnet localhost ftp

# /etc/hosts.deny - the syntax is <DAEMON>:<CLIENT>
vsftpd: ALL

# check after
$ telnet localhost ftp

# You should get a connection refused message. Note: The loopback may still work, use a different adapter.
# Remove the line from /etc/hosts.deny to clean up the exercise

When traffic comes to a libwrap-enabled daemon, those two files are consulted to see the following:

    If the pattern matches in /etc/hosts.allow, the traffic is permitted.
    If the pattern is not found in /etc/hosts.allow, and it matches in /etc/hosts.deny, the traffic will be denied.
    If the pattern does not match in either file, the traffic will be permitted.

Let's make customizations on ssh!

- Each time we connect, on any domain, we want to use X11 Tunneling

A forward DNS query uses A (ipv4) or AAAA (ipv6) record types and its answer turns a DNS name into an IP address.

A reverse DNS query uses PTR record types and its answer turns an IP address into a DNS name, or more precisely in a :

    in-addr.arpa. domain for IPv4
    ip6.arpa. domain for IPv6

So, how can we make such queries?

$ dig A [domain_name]

$ dig -x [ipv4_or_ipv6_address]
$ nslookup -type=PTR [ipv4_or_ipv6_address]

- We say query (/ˈkwɪri/), but we can also say lookup or resolution

- The original use of the rDNS: network troubleshooting via $ traceroute, $ ping, and the "Received:" trace header field for SMTP e-mail etc
How can we define a r/w master (primary) DNS zone and a read-only slave (secondary) zone, serving as backup?

# named.conf

zone "example.com." IN {
   type master;
   file "example.com.zone";
}; 

zone "foo.example." IN {
   type slave;
   masters { 192.168.122.11; 192.168.131.45; };
};
The SOA (Start of Authority) is a DNS record required for every zone, containing administrative information about the zone, especially regarding zone transfers. Let's explain this one.

# BEWARE! 
Zone files are sensitive to spaces in the first character position.
# Yes, for some reason the ")" is before "; neg ttl"

@ IN SOA  ns1.example.com. john\.doe.example.com. ( 
2012012301 ; serial
3H        ; refresh
1H        ; retry
2H        ; expire
1M)       ; neg ttl

- The @ character expands to whatever the zone name is

- Zone class (IN = Internet)

- Primary name server

- Email address of administrator (john\.doe.example.com. = john.doe@example.com)

- Serial number (increments at each change - format: YYYYMMDDXX, where XX is a two-digit revision number)

- Timers (slave server refresh settings)
    Refresh: How often to check for new serial from master.
    Retry: How often to retry if no response from master.
    Expire: How long to keep returning authoritative answers when we cannot reach the master server.
    Negative TTL: How long to cache an NX (non existing) domain answer - that is to hold onto a "this record does not exist" answer.

Create an authoritative DNS forward zone for the example.com domain with the following settings

• www.example.com has the address 192.168.111.45 and the IPv6 address fe80::22c9:d0ff:1ecd:c0ef

• foo.example.com has the address 192.168.121.11

• bar.example.com has a CNAME pointing to www.example.com

• host1.example.com through host100.example.com have the addresses 10.20.45.1 through 10.20.45.100

Then, test 'em all!

# /var/named/example.com.zone
$TTL 30
@ IN SOA localhost. admin.example.com. (
2012092901 ; serial YYYYMMDDRR format
3H; refresh
1H; retry
2H; expire
1M); neg ttl
IN NS localhost.;
www.example.com. IN A 192.168.111.45
www.example.com. IN AAAA fe80::22c9:d0ff:1ecd:c0ef
foo.example.com. IN A 192.168.121.11
bar.example.com. IN CNAME www.example.com.
;generate one hundred entries host1 thru host100
$GENERATE 1-100 host$.example.com. IN A 10.20.45.$

# /etc/named.conf
zone "example.com." IN {
   type master;
   file "example.com.zone";
};

# equivalent to named checkconf -z
$ named checkzone

$ systemctl restart named

# do it for each record type
# check the 'QUESTION SECTION', or use +short (empty == no record found!):
$ dig @localhost -t A www.example.com
$ dig @localhost -t AAAA www.example.com
$ dig @localhost -t CNAME bar.example.com
Let's create an authoritative reverse DNS zone for the 10.20.45.0/255.255.255.0 network.

# /var/named/45.20.10.in-addr.arpa.zone
$TTL 30
@ IN SOA localhost. admin.example.com. (
2012092901 ; serial YYYYMMDDRR format
3H; refresh
1H; retry
2H; expire
1M); neg ttl
@ IN NS localhost.;
;generate 1-254
$GENERATE 1-254 $ IN PTR host$.example.com.

# /etc/named.conf
zone "45.20.10.in-addr.arpa." IN {
   type master;
   file "45.20.10.in-addr.arpa.zone";
}; 

$ named checkzone

$ rndc reload

$ host 10.20.45.anything localhost

$ mysqldump [database] --verbose | php -r '$z = new ZipArchive();$z->open($argv[1],ZipArchive::CREATE);$z->addFromString($argv[2],file_get_contents("php://stdin"));$z->close();' [archive.zip] [database.sql]

Let's show the difference between a name-based and a IP-based virtual host

# create a new virtual network interface (= IP alias for your main Ethernet address) and serve a different document root from the new interface
$ ip addr add 192.168.153.4/24 dev eth0

# /etc/httpd.conf  (if there's no such thing as Listen *:80)
Listen 192.168.153.4:80

# /etc/httpd/conf.d/ipvhost.conf
<VirtualHost 192.168.153.4:80>
   DocumentRoot /ipvhost/
   ServerName ipvhost.example.com
   <Directory /ipvhost/>
      Options Indexes FollowSymLinks
      AllowOverride None
      Require all granted
   </Directory>
</VirtualHost>

# /etc/httpd/conf.d/namevhost.conf
<VirtualHost *:80>
   DocumentRoot /namevhost/
   ServerName namevhost.example.com
   ServerAlias alias.example.com
  <Directory /namevhost/>
      Options Indexes FollowSymLinks
      AllowOverride None
      Require all granted
  </Directory>
</VirtualHost>

IP-based virtual hosts use the IP address of the connection to determine the correct virtual host to serve. Therefore you need to have a separate IP address for each host.

With name-based virtual hosting, the server relies on the client to report the hostname as part of the HTTP headers.
- Different hosts can share the same IP address
- Eases the demand for scarce IP addresses.

Therefore you should use name-based virtual hosting unless you are using equipment that explicitly demands IP-based hosting

netfilter decides what to do (policies) according to:

- Tables, made of...
- Chains, made of...
- Rules, made of...
- Match criteria + target (ACCEPT / DROP / DNAT / SNAT)

When no rules match, the chain falls back to the default policy!
 
Now, let's describe the chains composing the filter table and the NAT table. (There is also the mangle table! It is basically used to set specific headers for IP packets to affect the routing decision made further on... the TTL option is probably the most interesting!)

FILTER TABLE chains:
- INPUT (packets coming into the network, bound to local processes)
- OUTPUT (packets originated in the local network/processes, to be sent outside)
- FORWARD (packets which should be forwarded to another destination, as in the case of a router)

NAT TABLE chains:
- PREROUTING - For altering packets just as they come (before routing)
- OUTPUT - For altering packets generated by local processes, prior to routing
- POSTROUTING - For packets just about to go out to the network (after routing)

- Anyway, remember these commands have different goals! wget is a tool to download (recursively) files from servers; curl is a tool that let's you exchange requests/responses with a server

Postfix daemon does not provide a Simple Authentication and Security Layer (SASL) mechanism itself, but supports the Dovecot SASL mechanisms. So:

- Configure Dovecot and the Dovecot SASL system.

- Configure Postfix to use the Dovecot SASL system for authentication, to trust users who have properly authenticated and set up the proper auth path.

- Test!

# /etc/dovecot/conf.d/10-master.conf
unix_listener /var/spool/postfix/private/auth {
   mode = 0666
}

$ postconf -e "smtpd_sasl_type = dovecot"
$ postconf -e "smtpd_sasl_auth_enable = yes"
$ postconf -e "smtpd_sasl_path = private/auth"
# wanna test on the local machine? omit permit_mynetworks
$ postconf -e "smtpd_recipient_restrictions = permit_mynetworks, permit_sasl_authenticated, reject_unauth_destination, reject"

A Virtual Local Area Network (VLAN) is a method for combining two or more separated LANs to appear as the same LAN. It must be configured both at the network switch and in each host!

Create a VLAN-trunked interface:

- Enable VLANs on the current host

- Create a VLAN interface configuration file with ID 7, having the the main interface ( eth0 ) as physical interface, with IP 192.168.X.100 , where X is a number unique to your classroom/lab and 255.255.255.0 as netmask

- Start the newly defined interface

# /etc/sysconfig/network
VLAN=yes
VLAN_NAME_TYPE="DEV_PLUS_VID"

/etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-<INTERFACE>.7
DEVICE=<INTERFACE>.7
BOOTPROTO=static
TYPE=vlan
ONBOOT=yes
IPADDR=192.168.X.100
NETMASK=255.255.255.0
PHYSDEV="<INTERFACE>"

$ ifup <INTERFACE>.7

Create a guest-access Samba share. 

- Share the directory /home/export/cifs as the share name "mainexports":

• Use the workgroup name LNXFND .
• Allow read only, guest access to all hosts in the workgroup.
• Allow public access.

- Check if the share is available, both from localhost and from a remote machine

# Backup smb.conf
$ mv /etc/samba/smb.conf /etc/samba/smb.conf.backup

# smb.conf 
[global]
workgroup = LNXFND
server string = Myserver
log file = /var/log/samba/log.%m
max log size = 50
cups options = raw
[mainexports]
path = /home/export/cifs
read only = yes
guest ok = yes
comment = Main exports share

$ systemctl reload smb

# if you forget -N iw will ask for a password, but you can just press 'enter' and go on as anonymous, 
$ smbclient -L 127.0.0.1 -N
$ smbclient //SERVER/mainexports

- If there is a user id and password added by smbpasswd, you may use those credentials. 

- $ smbclient is for temporary access, but of course you can $ mount -t cifs the share, use /etc/fstab or automount

- Always remember: for the same directory, of course we can have a set of UNIX privileges... And a whole different set of Samba privileges!

On a Linux system, when changing the ownership of a symbolic link using chown, by default it changes the target of the symbolic link (ie, whatever the symbolic link is pointing to).

If you'd like to change ownership of the link itself, you need to...

# --no-dereference
$ chown -h [owner]: [file]

The default targets in the netfilter system are:

    - ACCEPT      Pass the packet along to the next stage
    - DROP          Send no response to this packet and ignore it
    - RETURN     Go back to the calling CHAIN and start processing on the next rule
    - REJECT      Send a message back explaining why the packet is not allowed (better for debugging!)

What shall then be difference in this two cases?

Remeber the syntax is:

# iptables -A [CHAIN] [criteria] -j [TARGET]

$ iptables -A INPUT -p icmp -i eth0 -j DROP

# flush between the two!
# $ iptables -F INPUT

$ iptables -A INPUT -p icmp -i eth0 -j REJECT

Using TCP wrappers, lock down all services except sshd when the connection is to the IPV6 loopback.

Oct 10 07:57:13 centos root: libwrap: From ::1, To ::1, Daemon sshd, client (ˈklaɪənt/) unknown

- Restrict ssh to only use the IPv6 loopback [::1] address. Log the successes and failures. 
This should work and log a success: $ ssh student@::1
This should fail and log a failure: $ ssh student@127.0.0.1

# /etc/hosts.allow
ALL:All:spawn (/bin/logger libwrap\: From %a, To %A, Daemon %d, client (ˈklaɪənt/) %u) &
$ tail -f /var/log/messages

# /etc/hosts.allow
sshd: [::1]/64 :spawn (/bin/logger libwrap\: ALLOWED From %a, To %A, Daemon %d, client (ˈklaɪənt/) %u) &
# /etc/hosts.deny
ALL:All:spawn (/bin/logger libwrap\: Denied From %a, To %A, Daemon %d, client (ˈklaɪənt/) %u) &
Using the conntrack / state module to write stateful firewall rules, what are the possible connection states tracked by iptables?

New: the incoming packet is not part of any existing connection (stream or socket) and the TCP SYN flag is set. 

Established: the incoming packet belongs to an existing connection (stream or socket); the SYN flag is OFF and at least one other TCP flag (ACK, RST) is set. 

Related: the incoming packet is not part of any existing stream or socket, but is expected because a socket anticipates it. Example: 
– Data packets on port 20 when an FTP control connection exists on port 21. 

Invalid: if none of the above states apply, the packet state is INVALID. This occurs with fraudulent access attempts or simply when unexpected packets arrive outside the CONNTRACK rules.
A network namespace is logically another copy of the network stack, with its own routes, firewall rules, and network devices. Let's do it.

# create it ("netns1")
$ ip netns add netns1

# verify the available network devices within the namespace
$ ip netns exec netns1 ip link list

# the 'lo' interface is configured but is not up. Set it up, verify it's up, check its IP address and ping it
$ ip netns exec netns1 ip link set dev lo up
$ ip netns exec netns1 ip a
$ ip netns exec netns1 ping -c3 127.0.0.1

# create a pair of virtual adapters (device pair) to communicate between the namespace network stack and the default network stack
$ ip link add veth0 type veth peer name veth1

# connect the second virtual adapter to the new namespace, assign it an IP address
$ ip link set veth1 netns netns1
$ ip netns exec netns1 ifconfig veth1 10.1.1.1/24 up

# assign the first one an IP address in the default namespace
$ ifconfig veth0 10.1.1.2/24 up

# use tcpdump to monitor the traffic to and from the namespace adapter, and use ping on one of the devices
$ tcpdump -i veth0 -n -B512
$ ping -c3 10.1.1.1
veth devices are always created in interconnected pairs. Packets transmitted on one device in the pair are immediately received on the other device.

Find and perform an action (remove in an interactive way) on some files. These all seem fine, BUT...

# can be unwieldy (cumbersome) to type
$ find . -wholename "*~" -exec rm -i {} \;

# will fail with filenames containing blank spaces
$ find . -wholename "*~" | xargs rm -i
$ for names in $(find . -wholename "*~" ) ; do rm -i $names ; done
$ find . -wholename "*~" -print0 | xargs -0 rm -i

The find utility is using ; or + to terminate the shell commands invoked by -exec. So to avoid special shell characters from interpretation, they need to be escaped with a backslash to remove any special meaning for the next character read and for line continuation. Therefore all the following syntaxes are allowed: 

find . -exec echo {} \;
find . -exec echo {} ';'
find . -exec echo {} ";"
find . -exec echo {} \+
find . -exec echo {} +

We need -wholename (instead of a simple -name) when we look for more than a basename, that is a path containing slashes.
Suppose I want to quickly add (append) some lines to a file, in direct or reverse order. This is the way to go, and we quit/save by Ctrl+D. A famous alternative is...

$ cat >[>] [file]

$ tac >[>] [file]

# quit by writing EOF at the beginning of a line

$ cat >[>] [file] << EOF

$ tac >[>] [file] << EOF
Bash has no switch! But...

#!/bin/sh
echo "Do you want to destroy your entire file system?"
read -r response

case "$response" in
   "y" | "Y" | "YES" | "yes" ) echo "I hope you know what you are doing!" ;
                       echo ’I am going to type: " rm -rf /"’;;
   "n" | "N" | "NO" | "no" )  echo "You have some comon sense!" ;;
   *   )               echo "You have to give an answer!" ;;
esac
exit 0

Btw... The -r option to read prevents backslash interpretation (usually used as a backslash newline pair, to continue over multiple lines or to escape the delimiters). Without this option, any unescaped backslashes in the input will be discarded. You should almost always use the -r option with read.

There are quite a few queues in Postfix:

- incoming (inbound)
- active (messages the queue manager has opened for delivery --> limited, according to leaky bucket strategy, for a fixed delivery rate)
- deferred (could not be delivered upon the first attempt --> exponential backoff by doubling the time between delivery attempts)
- corrupt (unreadable or damaged, up for inspection)
- hold (waiting for someone to set them free)

Assuming we just need a general view of the queues...

- Visualize 'em

- Remove messages according to domain (probably a spammer!)

- Read a specific message that is still in the queue... or that has left the queue

- Process the queue now

# equivalent to $ postqueue -p
$ mailq

# https://serverfault.com/questions/638152/how-to-remove-postfix-queue-messages-sent-to-a-specific-domain
$ mailq | tail -n +2 | awk 'BEGIN { RS = "" } /@example\.com/ { print $1 }' | tr -d '*!' | sudo postsuper -d -

$ postcat -q [hash_from_mailq]

# the mail_id is to be taken from the mail log 
$ grep -rl [[mail_id] [/path/to/account/Maildir]
$ cat [result_of_previous_command]

$ postqueue -f

- Yes, just use "cat"! "postcat" will give crazy fatal erros about "invalid size record"!

- If you need to zoom in more precisely on one of the queues, use $ qshape [active | deferred ... ]

Split a single FLAC file in multiple tracks, according to its CUE sheet metadata.

Use Flacon :

- "Scan" a source, to search in multiple sub-directories

- Make sure that "Directory" of the destination is "Same as CUE file" and probably the "Pattern" should NOT have slashes, to keep things compact

- "Convert"

Batch convert

- APE to FLAC 

- FLAC to OGG

- WAV to MP3

- MP3 to OGG

# the genral fast syntax is always $ ffmpeg -i musicfile.original musicfile.destination
# always use the custom parameters after -i file_source

$ find . -name "*.ape" -exec sh -c 'exec ffmpeg -i "$1" "${1%.ape}.flac"' _ {} \;

$ find . -name "*.flac" -exec sh -c 'exec ffmpeg -i "$1" "${1%.flac}.ogg"' _ {} \;

$ find . -name "*.wav" -exec sh -c 'exec ffmpeg -i "$1" -vn -ar 44100 -ac 2 -b:a 192k  "${1%.wav}.mp3"' _ {} \;

$ find . -name "*.mp3" -exec sh -c 'exec ffmpeg -i "$1" -acodec libvorbis  "${1%.mp3}.ogg"' _ {} \;

# Remove the original files? Change -ok to -exec if you don't want no confirmation; change the extension accordingly
$ find . -name "*.ape" -ok rm {} ';'

-vn --> Disable video, to make sure no video (including album cover image) is included if the source would be a video file.

-ar --> Set the audio sampling frequency. For output streams it is set by default to the frequency of the corresponding input stream. For input streams this option only makes sense for audio grabbing devices and raw demuxers and is mapped to the corresponding demuxer options.

-ac --> Set the number of audio channels. For output streams it is set by default to the number of input audio channels. For input streams this option only makes sense for audio grabbing devices and raw demuxers and is mapped to the corresponding demuxer options. So used here to make sure it is stereo (2 channels)

-b:a --> Converts the audio bitrate to be exact 192kbit per second

How should I configute the firewall of a host system so to allow connections to a service (like FTP or SSH) in a virtual machine?

# host - firewall for SSH
$ iptables -t nat -I PREROUTING -p tcp -d [host_address] --dport [any_local_port] -j DNAT --to [VM_local_address]:22

# test from the outside world
$ ssh [host_address] -p [local_port_used_above]

# host - FTP with passive ports (also configured on the FTP server of the virtual machine)
$ iptables -t nat -I PREROUTING -p tcp -d [host_address] --dport 21 -j DNAT --to [VM_local_address]:21
$ iptables -t nat -I PREROUTING -p tcp -d [host_address] --dport [start_passive_port]:[end_passive_port] -j DNAT --to [VM_local_address]

# check prerouting chain: "dpt" is the port you're looking for
$ iptables -L -n -t nat

Of course, we are assuming that in the virtual machine FTP/SSH are configured on ports 21/22.

See card on the NAT table (prerouting chain) for some theory

Delete a rule?

You have installed proFTPd. With all likelihood, before using it you would need to enable passive ports. And then...

# /etc/proftpd/proftpd.conf
PassivePorts 60000 60030

# check syntax and restart
$ proftpd -td10
$ systemctl restart proftpd.service

# enable module
$ modprobe ip_conntrack_ftp

# allow ftp connections on port 21
$ iptables -A INPUT  -p tcp -m tcp --dport 21 -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,NEW -j ACCEPT
$ iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -m tcp --dport 21 -m conntrack --ctstate NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT

# allow passive inboud connections 
$ iptables -A INPUT  -p tcp -m tcp --sport 49152:65534 --dport 49152:65534 -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
$ iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -m tcp --sport 49152:65534 --dport 49152:65534 -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

- Reference: https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/93554/iptables-to-allow-incoming-ftp

- You won't see the ports on the iptables chain unless proftpd is active and running (ps aux | grep -i ftp)

Time to certificate.

- Issue a new one and install it to Apache (/əˈpætʃi/) or Nginx with the webroot method (place files in a server's webroot folder for authentication)

- List all certificates

- Manually renew it (will be auto-renewed every 60 days)

- Revoke it and remove its traces

This is the Acme.sh way. What about Certbot?

### Acme ###

$ acme.sh --issue -d [domain] -d [www.domain] -w [website_directory]
$ acme.sh --install-cert -d [domain] ---reloadcmd "[systemctl reload apache2 | nginx]"

$ acme.sh --list

$ acme.sh --renew -d [domain]
$ acme.sh --renew-all

$ acme.sh --revoke -d [domain] && rm -r ~/.acme.sh/[domain]

### Certbot ###

# Different instructions for different platforms! See: https://certbot.eff.org/instructions

$ certbot certificates

$ certbot certonly --webroot -w /var/www/ssl/ -d [domain] -n --agree-tos --force-renewal --cert-name [domain] --post-hook "systemctl reload h2o"
$ certbot renew --post-hook "systemctl reload h2o"

# Ensure the auto-renewal is working
$ certbot renew --dry-run

# Yes, you need both! 
$ certbot revoke --cert-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/CERTNAME/cert.pem 
$ certbot delete --cert-name [domain]

--agree-tos Agree to the ACME server's Subscriber Agreement

-cert-name Makes Certbot overwrite the existing entry instead of creating a new one

-n Run non-interactively

- This is all based on Let's Encrypt. Wanna debug some domain? Try:
https://letsdebug.net/
https://unboundtest.com/

The Intelligent Input Bus (IBus, pronounced as I-Bus) is an input method (IM) framework for multilingual input in Unix-like operating-systems, than can be extended with plugins providing different input method engines (IME), like Anthy (package ibus-anthy) or Google's mozc (package ibus-mozc), if we're talking 'bout the Japanese Language.

Now, to actually use it...

Gnome (tried on both Fedora and Ubuntu):

- Add "Japanese (Kana and Kanji)" in the Input Sources of Region and Language (Gnome). Anthy should be the deafault IME

- Switch with Win + Space

- You may need to change the input method too, by choosing Hiragan on the upper right menu

- After typing the hiragana, press Space to see the Kanji alternatives

JPEG 2000, JPEG XR, and WebP are image formats that have superior compression and quality characteristics compared to their older JPEG and PNG counterparts.

WebP is widely supported and provides better lossy and lossless compression for images on the web. Here we are generating the images... How to use 'em?

# convert (by cloning, not overwriting) all images in sub-directories
# the original owner will be preserved! :-)
# in Debian, the package is simply "webp"
$ find ./ -type f -name '*.png' -exec sh -c 'cwebp -q 75 $1 -o "${1%.png}.webp"' _ {} \;
$ find ./ -type f -name '*.jpg' -exec sh -c 'cwebp -q 75 $1 -o "${1%.jpg}.webp"' _ {} \;

# See related card for Gulpfile

# .htaccess

          ##### TRY FIRST the file appended with .webp (ex. test.jpg.webp) ##### 
          # Does browser explicitly support webp? 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_USER_AGENT} Chrome [OR] 
          # OR Is request from Page Speed 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_USER_AGENT} "Google Page Speed Insights" [OR] 
          # OR does this browser explicitly support webp 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_ACCEPT} image/webp 
          # AND NOT MS EDGE 42/17 - doesnt work. 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_USER_AGENT} !Edge/17 
          # AND is the request a jpg or png? 
          RewriteCond %{REQUEST_URI} ^(.+)\.(?:jpe?g|png)$ 
          # AND does a .ext.webp image exist? 
          RewriteCond %{DOCUMENT_ROOT}%{REQUEST_URI}.webp -f 
          # THEN send the webp image and set the env var webp 
          RewriteRule ^(.+)$ $1.webp [NC,T=image/webp,E=webp,L] 

          ##### IF NOT, try the file with replaced extension (test.webp) ##### 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_USER_AGENT} Chrome [OR] 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_USER_AGENT} "Google Page Speed Insights" [OR] 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_ACCEPT} image/webp 
          RewriteCond %{HTTP_USER_AGENT} !Edge/17 
          # AND is the request a jpg or png? (also grab the basepath %1 to match in the next rule) 
          RewriteCond %{REQUEST_URI} ^(.+)\.(?:jpe?g|png)$ 
          # AND does a .ext.webp image exist? 
          RewriteCond %{DOCUMENT_ROOT}/%1.webp -f 
          # THEN send the webp image and set the env var webp 
          RewriteRule (.+)\.(?:jpe?g|png)$ $1.webp [NC,T=image/webp,E=webp,L]


# HTML: browsers that don't support <picture> will just display whatever source is specified in the <img> tag
# But... You just need the .htaccess above
<figure>
 <picture>
   <source srcset="img/awesomeWebPImage.webp" type="image/webp">
    <source srcset="img/creakyOldJPEG.jpg" type="image/jpeg"> 
   <img  onerror="onImageError.call(this)" src="img/creakyOldJPEG.jpg" alt="Alt Text!">
 </picture>
</figure>

# CSS: a Modernizr.webp build will automatically add one of two classes to the <html> element: webp or no-webp. Then, just use both...
.no-webp .elementWithBackgroundImage {
  background-image: url("image.jpg");
}

.webp .elementWithBackgroundImage{
  background-image: url("image.webp");
}

# JS - Handle the case of 404 errors
function onImageError() {
    this.onerror = null;
    this.parentNode.children[0].srcset = this.parentNode.children[1].srcset = this.src;
}
With the .htaccess you won't actually need the HTML and CSS ones.

From SO: It's high time to solve this conundrum that's been bothering me for years...

What's the right way to quote $(command $arg)?

That is, we want to prevent word globbing (using wildcard characters that shouldn't be used) and word splitting in a script.
"$(command "$arg")"

In general, quotes don't nest in bash; but in this case, the $( ) creates a new context, so the quotes inside it are independent of the quotes outside it. And you generally want quotes in both contexts. For a lengthy explanation: https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/443990
Use shell variables within a script?

$ awk -v awk_month="$month" -v awk_year="$year" [etc]

# check if the third field has a format number/awk_month/awk_year
# see: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56985559/shell-variables-within-match-with-a-date-regex
match($3, sprintf("^[0-9]+\\/%s\\/%s", awk_last_month, awk_year))
Warning. Escape sequences will be interpreted so \t becomes a real tab, and not "\t" if that is what you're looking for. Can be solved by using ENVIRON[] or access it via ARGV[]

Scan a directory (or a file, including archives) for malware?

### ClamScan AntiVirus (AV) ###

$ sudo freshclam # see notes below in case of error
$ sudo clamscan -i --recursive [dir] | tee clamscan.log # don't forget the -i, or it won't print the infected files at all!

### Linux Malware Detect (LMD) ###

# Complete
$ maldet --scan-all [dir_to_scan]

# Last X days
$ maldet -r [dir_to_scan] [X]

# Whitelisting - /usr/local/maldetect/ignore_paths
[/some/full/path]

- Use both maldet and the clamav engine (already set to be used by the default configuration in /usr/local/maldetect/conf.maldet), for improved performance and security

- Use both the client (ˈklaɪənt/) (clamav) and the daemons (clamav-daemon and clamav-freshclam). The freshclam service (virus DB upgrading) must be started before (and wait a minute or so...) clamav-daemon, or you'll get an error like ConditionPathExistsGlob=/var/lib/clamav/daily.{c[vl]d,inc} was not met

- How to avoid clamdscan's “ERROR: Can't access file [dead link]” in /usr/local/maldetect/logs? Then check these parameters: $ grep -i symlinks /etc/clamav/clamd.conf

- By default, Maldect checks the /tmp directory. Remember to comment that out if you don't need it.

- ERROR: /var/log/clamav/freshclam.log is locked by another process
You don't have to run it manually because it has been run automatically and is running in the background, that's why you receive that message. If you want to stop the daemon and run it manually: sudo systemctl stop clamav-freshclam.service

/etc/aliases specifies mail aliases used by MTAs. For example, mail to the ftp account would be sent to root's mailbox. And we could specifiy an actual e-mail address instead of "root"! Now, what should we do after upgrading it?

www: root

If using Postfix as MTA, the alias database is not in /etc/aliases. To find out the location for your system:

$ postconf alias_maps
How can I list all account?

# /etc/postfix/main.cf
virtual_alias_maps = proxy:mysql:/etc/postfix/virtual/forwarding.cf, proxy:mysql:/etc/postfix/virtual/mailbox.cf

- We have found out we're using Postfix-mysql

- Check out those files, /etc/postfix/virtual/mailbox.cf and /etc/postfix/virtual/forwarding.cf

One bird, two stones (or what is the other way around?)...

- Your system is "mute". After executing: $ echo "test mail" | mail -v -s Subject root', root (or maybe the external mail configured in /etc/aliases) does not receive anything.

- You do send, but in the logs: email@domain.tld R=smarthost T=remote_smtp_smarthost: message is too big (transport limit = 1) 

What now?

# /etc/exim4/update-exim4.conf.conf - yes, in Debian it's .conf.conf
dc_eximconfig_configtype='internet'
IGNORE_SMTP_LINE_LENGTH_LIMIT=1

$ systemctl reload exim4
BTW, unlike qmail, Postfix, and ZMailer, Exim does not have a central queue manager: "thundering herd problem" when multiple messages addressed to a single domain are submitted at once!

Now, supposing that we don't have an installed MTA or anyway we don't want these mails at all, but still be able to log...

[error] [pid 3016] mod_authz_core.c(873): [client 93.66.41.64:41618] AH01630: client denied by server configuration: .../require/dynamic.php
But we know that's a lecit request! Let's allow that file.

And from the access log, something like this:
"POST /xmlrpc.php HTTP/1.1" 200 528 "-" "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Ubuntu; Linux x86_64; rv:62.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/62.0"
But we don't want it! How to block access to /xmlrpc.php (for each website?)

<Directory "/var/www/.../require">
   <Files "dynamic.php">
      Require all granted
   </Files>
</Directory>

<LocationMatch "/xmlrpc.php">

</LocationMatch>

The directive <LocationMatch> behaves identical to the regex version of <Location>. According to the Apache (/əˈpætʃi/) doc: "is preferred, for the simple reason that ~ is hard to distinguish from - in many fonts".

We could mount a remote NFS share on-demand with mount or permanently through the /etc/fstab file, but the downside is that the system needs our intervention or must allocate the necessary resources to keep the share mounted at all times. An alternative is to mount the desired file system on-demand automatically through autofs (autofs package) which can mount file systems when they are used and unmount them after a period of inactivity. How?

# /etc/auto.master
/media/nfs /etc/auto.nfs-share --timeout=60

# /etc/auto.nfs-share
# these are subdirectories in /media/nfs created dynamically by autofs
writeable_share -fstype=nfs4 192.168.0.10:/ 
non_writeable_share -fstype=nfs4 192.168.0.10:/mydir

$ systemctl restart autofs
Let's explain what's going on with this NFS export.

# /etc/exports
/NFS-SHARE 192.168.0.17(fsid=0,no_subtree_check,rw,root_squash,sync,anonuid=1000,anongid=1000)

• rw (short for read-write): Allows remote hosts to make write changes in the exported file systems.
  • sync: The NFS server replies to requests only after changes have been committed to permanent storage (i.e., the hard disk). Its opposite, the async option, may increase performance but at the cost of data loss or corruption after an unclean server restart.
  • root_squash: Prevents remote root users from having superuser privileges in the server and assigns them the user ID for user nobody. If you want to “squash” all users (and not just root), you can use the all_squash option.
  • anonuid / anongid: Explicitly sets the UID and GID of the anonymous account (nobody).
  • subtree_check: If only a subdirectory of a file system is exported, this option verifies that a requested file is located in that exported subdirectory. On the other hand, if the entire file system is exported, disabling this option with no_subtree_check will speed up transfers. The default option nowadays is no_subtree_check as subtree checking tends to cause more problems than it is worth, according to man 5 exports.
  • fsid=0 | root (zero or root): Specifies that the specified file system is the root of multiple exported directories (only applies in NFSv4).
Disable or enable the usage of SSH from the current machine (to other machines)... And vice versa! This is a stateless implementation. What about a stateful one?

# SSH from current machine
$ iptables -P OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j REJECT
$ iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -o lo -j ACCEPT # whitelist loopback, required by some applications

# SSH to current machine
$ iptables -P INPUT -p tcp -s [IP] --dport 22 -j REJECT
$ iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -i lo --dport 22 -j ACCEPT # whitelist loopback, or a specific IP with -s

$ iptables -P OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j REJECT
$ iptables -I OUTPUT 1 -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT # not SSH-specific!
$ iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT

$ iptables -P INPUT -p tcp -s [IP] --dport 22 -j REJECT
$ iptables -I INPUT 1 -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT # not SSH-specific! 
$ iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT

- We don't specify the table (-t): by default it's filter

- In the stateless connections, we don't specify the module (-m), as part of the match criteria, because when when using -p or --protocol, the corresponding module (/ˈmɑːdʒuːl/) is loaded automagically.

- Yes, there are S.O. answers talking about --sport, but we tried and that was not the case!

- The conntrack module supersedes state, but in modern kernels there is now no difference between the two, so the syntax -m state --state is actually translated into -m conntrack --ctstate and handled by the same module . In older Kernels, you have to specify you're using conntrack!

- Of course the sshd binded port could be different from 22! Check it.

-o is the name of an interface via which a packet is going to be sent (for packets entering the FORWARD, OUTPUT and POSTROUTING chains), while -i is the name of an interface via which a packet was received (only for
packets entering the INPUT, FORWARD and PREROUTING chains)
Allowing / preventing NFS clients (from 192.168.0.0/24) to mount NFS4 shares on ports 2049 and 111.
$ mount -t nfs4 192.168.0.10:/ /mnt

# flush, if needed: $ iptables -F 

# open traffic (of course, just change target to close... Ang get a "Connection timed out" error)
$ iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -s 0/0 -p tcp --dport 111 -j ACCEPT
$ iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -s 0/0 -p tcp --dport 2049 -j ACCEPT
This is how we can make firewall rules persistent. But it stills requires some manual work. What about full automatization?

# Debian
$ iptables-save > /etc/iptables/rules.v4

$ iptables-restore < [file_above]

# Debian/Ubuntu: this will load the rules saved in the /etc/iptables/rules.v4 file
$ apt-get install iptables-persistent

# CentOs: /etc/sysconfig/iptables-config
IPTABLES_SAVE_ON_STOP="yes" 
IPTABLES_SAVE_ON_RESTART="yes"

Linux Containers (LXC) is a lightweight kernel-based virtualization solution allowing you to run multiple isolated Linux distributions at the same time. It is very fast compared to KVM, XEN or VMware.

# Epel Repository
$ yum install epel-release
$ yum install debootstrap perl libvirt lxc lxc-templates

$ systemctl start lxc.service

$ systemctl start libvirtd

# check status of kernel virtualization
$ lxc-checkconfig

# create a new one from a container template in /usr/share/lxc/templates/
$ lxc-create -n [container_name] -t [container_template]

# list
$ lxc-ls 

# info
$ lxc-info [container_name]

# start the newly created container in background
$ lxc-start -n [container_name] -d

# list active
$ lxc-ls --active

# login (deatch with ctrl-a + q)
$ lxc-console -n [container_name]

# stop it
$ lxc-stop -n [container_name]

Dynamic Kernel module (/ˈmɑːdʒuːl/) Support (DKMS) is a framework that enables generating Linux kernel modules whose sources generally reside outside the kernel source tree. The concept is to have DKMS modules automatically rebuilt when a new kernel is installed.

#!/bin/bash

DIR=$1

# -If $? equals 0, add the file to the list of files to be backed up.

Let's explain these lines in /etc/sudoers

Defaults secure_path="/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin"
root ALL=(ALL) ALL
tecmint ALL=/bin/yum update
gacanepa ALL=NOPASSWD:/bin/updatedb
$admin ALL=(ALL) ALL

# Defaults secure_path="/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin"
This line lets you specify the directories that will be used for sudo, and is used to prevent using user-specific directories.

# root ALL=(ALL) ALL
Rule applies to all hosts; the user in the first column (root) can run commands with the privileges of any user; any command can be run

# tecmint ALL=/bin/yum update
If no user is specified after the = sign, sudo assumes the root user. In this case, user tecmint will be able to run yum update as root.

# gacanepa ALL=NOPASSWD:/bin/updatedb
The NOPASSWD directive allows user gacanepa to run /bin/updatedb without needing to enter his password.

# $admin ALL=(ALL) ALL
The % sign indicates that this line applies to a group called “admin”. This means that members of the group “admin” can run all commands as any user on all hosts.

How to run a cron...

- Every first Monday (or another day of the week) of every month

@reboot some-command

To check wheter SpamAssassin is actually working (with or without Amavis), we can use the Generic Test for Unsolicited Bulk Email (GTUBE) - https://spamassassin.apache.org/gtube/ - that is send a message to an account with this string in the body (the subject can be whatever): XJS*C4JDBQADN1.NSBN3*2IDNEN*GTUBE-STANDARD-ANTI-UBE-TEST-EMAIL*C.34X

Now...
$ cat /var/log/mail.log | grep -i spam
If you're using Amavis, this should get (both for incoming and outgoing mails) a Passed SPAM instead of Passed SPAMMY or Passed CLEAN

You're testing Amavis / SpamAssassin (see related card) and you realize that messages have been marked as spam, but still having the tags:

{RelayedTaggedInbound}
{RelayedOutbound,Quarantined}

Meaning that they do get received (even if tagged) and sent (even if quarantined). What about it?

# one file in: /etc/amavis/conf.d/20-debian_default (or similar!)
# change D_PASS to...
$final_spam_destiny = D_BOUNCE;

# You should know see:
Blocked SPAM {BouncedOpenRelay,Quarantined}
Blocked SPAM {BouncedOutbound,Quarantined}

D_PASS    Mail will pass to recipients, regardless of bad contents. Note that including a recipient in a @*_lovers_maps is functionally equivalent to setting $final_*_destiny = D_PASS; for that recipient. 

D_BOUNCE    Mail will not be delivered to its recipients. A non-delivery notification (bounce) will be created by amavisd-new and sent to the sender. 

D_REJECT    Mail will not be delivered to its recipients. Amavisd-new will send the typical 550 (or 554) reject response to the upstream MTA and that MTA may create a reject notice (bounce) and return it to the sender. This notice is not as informative as the one amavisd-new would create if you were using D_BOUNCE

D_DISCARD    Mail will not be delivered to its recipients and the sender normally will NOT be notified. 

For each of these, if a quarantine is configured, a copy of the mail will go there.

Good guide here: https://blog.bravi.org/?p=683

Due to the nature of LVM, we can easily reduce the size of the latter (say 2.5 GB) and allocate it for the former, while resizing each filesystem at the same time. That is, resize logical volumes

- It can happen that you arrive at a point when resizing logical volumes cannot solve your storage needs anymore and you need to buy an extra storage device. So, let's add another PV to the VG and thus extend volume groups

$ lvextend -l +100%FREE -r /dev/vg00/vol_backups

$ pvcreate /dev/sdd
$ vgextend vg00 /dev/sdd

This is how we could compare objectively measurable criteria of a video file, that is:

- Frame Rate (FPS)

- Video bitrate

- Resolution

But...

# we use 2>&1 because ffmpeg -i (or $ ffprobe) writes to stderr, not stdout
# the video resolution is on the same line of fps
$ ffmpeg -i  [file] 2>&1 | grep -E "fps|bitrate"

What you want is a program that gives you a Mean Opinion Score (MOS) of a video, i.e. a number between 1 and 5, or between 0 and 100, which corresponds to the quality as perceived by a human being, since:

- A 1080p movie rip at 700MB size might look worse than a 720p rip at 700MB, because for the former, the bitrate is too low, which introduces all kinds of compression artifacts.

- The same goes for comparing bitrate at similar frame sizes, as different encoders can actually deliver better quality at less bitrate, or vice-versa. For example, a 720p 700MB rip produced with XviD will look worse than a 700MB rip produced with x264, because the latter is much more efficient.

There's lot more to know! Check https://superuser.com/questions/338725/compare-two-video-files-to-find-out-which-has-best-quality

A core dump is a file containing a process's address space (memory) when the process terminates unexpectedly. Core dumps are triggered by the kernel in response to program crashes, and may be passed to a helper program for further processing. A core dump is not typically used by an average user, but may be passed on to developers upon request where it can be invaluable as a post-mortem snapshot of the program's state at the time of the crash, especially if the fault is hard to reliably reproduce.

Now, how to examine a coredump?

# list 
$ coredumpctl list

# examine one
$ coredumpctl info [PID]

# Pay attention to "Signal" row, that helps to identify crash cause. For deeper analysis you can examine the backtrace using gdb:
$ coredumpctl gdb match
