Describe in detail how the SysV Init process worked.

- The kernel looks for init in /sbin and launches it, handing over control of the boot phase (init becomes the parent of all processes).

- init reads /etc/inittab — which describes the configuration for each runlevel — and starts all background processes required by the system by scanning /etc/rcX.d (where X is the default runlevel from inittab). It stops all scripts whose name begins with K and starts all scripts whose name begins with S.

- /sbin/init then starts the system console processes (getty) and runs /etc/rc.d/rc.local.

Privacy-Enhanced Mail (PEM) is a de facto file format for storing and sending cryptographic keys, certificates, and other data, used also by Let's Encrypt. 

-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- 
MIICLDCCAdKgAwIBAgIBADAK...
-----END CERTIFICATE-----

Now, suppose you have received a key, crt and ca-bundle files and want to convert them to a private and public PEM files, to be used by Let's Encrypt.

# If the CRT and KEY files begin with "-----BEGIN" and you can read 'em in a text editor... It's already PEM! 
# In this case, just concatenate the CRT with the CA
# If the file is in binary... There's another card about that!
$ cat [cert.crt] [cer.ca-bundle] > cert.pem

A shebang - also called sha-bang, hashbang, pound-bang, or hash-pling is...

The character sequence at the beginning of a script consisting of the characters:

- number sign (#)

- exclamation mark (!)

Usually folloed by the shell in use, like #!/bin/bash
Iterating over ls output is fragile. Use globs!!

# Problematic code

for f in $(ls *.wav) do 
   [[ -e "$f" ]] || break # handle the case of no *.wav files
   echo "$f" 
done

# Correct code

for f in *.wav do
   [[ -e "$f" ]] || break # handle the case of no *.wav files 
   echo "$f" 
done

Command expansion causes word splitting and glob expansion, which will cause problems for certain filenames. The following files can or will break the first loop:

'filename with spaces.wav'
'filename with * globs.wav'
'More_Globs[2003].wav'
'files_with_fønny_chæracters_in_certain_locales.wav'

The first line of code looks like "print the first argument". It's actually "Split the first argument by IFS (spaces, tabs and line feeds). Expand each of them as if it was a glob. Join all the resulting strings and filenames with spaces. Print the result." The second one looks like "iterate through all arguments". It's actually "Split all the arguments by IFS. Expand each of them as globs, and iterate on the resulting list". 

Double quote variables to prevent globbing and word splitting!

# Problematic code 
echo $1
for i in $@; do :; done

# Correct code
echo "$1"
for i in "$@"; do :; done

Quoting variables prevents the script from breaking when input contains spaces, line feeds, glob characters and such. That also mean that when quoting composite arguments, make sure to exclude globs and brace expansions, which lose their special meaning in double quotes: "$HOME/$dir/src/*.c" will not expand, but "$HOME/$dir/src"/*.c will.

BTW... Historically, Bourne shells didn't have true and false as built-in commands. true was instead simply aliased to :, and false to something like let 0. So : is slightly better than true for portability to ancient Bourne-derived shells. 
Since if requires a non-empty then clause and comments don't count as non-empty, : serves as a no-op.
Only expansions need to be quoted, but for stylistic reasons entire arguments with multiple variable and literal parts are often quoted as one by using Canonical Quoting... How?

# Unquoted (bad) 
$HOME/$dir/dist/bin/$file 

# Minimal quoting (good, but not optimal) 
"$HOME"/"$dir"/dist/bin/"$file"

# Canonical quoting (optimal)
"$HOME/$dir/dist/bin/$file"

IFS ("internal field separator") is a built-in shell variable used to determine how to do word splitting, i. e. how to recognize word boundaries. By default (note the whitespace!) 

IFS=$' \t\n

We can change it, though, and thus prevent the following code from breaking, in the case the ls returns filenames with spaces whitin.

for i in $(ls -1); do 
        du -hs "$i";
done;

# save it
OLDIFS=$IFS

IFS=$'\t\n'; 
for i in $(ls -1); do 
        du -hs "$i"; 
done; 

# restore it
IFS="$OLDIFS"
Reading knowledge! You can do it in other ways too
How do you benchmark two different HTTP servers? The goal is to achieve high Requests/sec (k/s) and Transfer/sec (Mb/s), while minimising Average Latency (ms) and Socket Errors (such as Timeouts).

• loop from 1 to 100
• flush any buffered data to disk at each iteration
• use 12 threads 
• generate 400 connections
• run the test for 30 seconds (repeated request of the same page)
• page to test (use a different port for each of the two web servers)

Beyond Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM), which handles authentication for PAM-aware applications ($ ldd $(which [cmd]) | grep libpam), there is another authentication subsystem — independent of /etc/group, /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/gshadow — called NSS. Using the example below, how does it work?

for i in $(getent passwd | cut ­d: ­f1); do
   mkdir /scratch/$i
done;

Name Service Switch (NSS): a subsystem that provides applications with user data — and the mappings between users and other information — by querying system databases (passwd and group; via modules also MySQL or LDAP). The getent command retrieves entries from the NSS libraries.

In this example we extract the first field (username), but we could also target the gecos field (fifth field), which contains additional information such as the user's full name or phone number.

Linux-PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) separates authentication tasks into four independent management groups, each of which can define a sequence of operations through interchangeable modules:

– account: verifies any requirements the account must satisfy, e.g. whether it has expired or whether it has permission to log in. 

– auth: authenticates the user and acquires the necessary credentials. 

– password: updates or changes authentication information. A classic example is the password change operation. 

– session: manages the tasks that must be performed when opening and closing a session.

You probably have enabled a chroot jail (= used the DefaultRoot directive, to restrict FTP virtual users only to certain directories, that is their homes), but that means that by default you won't see any symlinks in the directories you connect to (http://www.proftpd.org/docs/howto/Chroot.html)! 

What about it?

$ mount --bind /var/ftp/incoming /home/bob/incoming 
$ mount --bind /var/ftp/incoming /home/dave/incoming

Now both bob and dave will see a "mount bind" called "incoming", functionally identical to a symlink!

Of course, if the target directory has a different owner, permissions will follow accordingly.
Docker images are usually described via Dockerfiles. And these are necessary when you want to customize a pulled image from a public registry. Now, how to actually build it, push it and run it?

# "Dockerifle" (tha't the file name)
FROM library/python:3.6.0-alpine # Creates a layer from a docker base image (based on alpine linux with python 3.6.0 pre-installed).
RUN pip install tornado # Install tornado library (command executed within the container)
COPY web-server.py /web-server.py # Copy the "web-server" python app from host to container
EXPOSE 8080 # best practice! won't actually publish the port (== make the container listen on the port), but it's a kind and nice documentation
CMD ["python", "/web-server.py"] # Default execution command for this image (again, command executed within the container)

# build for private use, and run in daemon mode
$ docker build -t py-web-server:v1 [dir_with_Dockerfile]
$ docker run -d py-web-server

#  build for a registry (Google Cloud Registry), push and run remotely
$ gcloud builds submit --tag gcr.io/${GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT}/quickstart-image .
$ docker push [URL]
$ docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --name py-web-server gcr.io/$PROJECT_ID/py-web-server:v1

- To make a port available outside of Docker, or to Docker containers in another network use the --publish or -p flag. This creates a firewall rule which maps a container port to a port on the Docker 

- Dockerfiles are not the only way to build a container! We could use a build configuration file (.yaml), and add other actions (testing, pushing, deployments) within the same file!

- If we avoid the tagging, we can build and run in one command: docker run --rm -it $(docker build -q .)

- Important command to know: WORKDIR (path where to execute CMD, RUN and COPY), but usually should not be specified (KISS)

- Wanna use a specific Dockerfile? Here (yes, you still need the dot): $ docker build -f [Dockerfile] .

- Authorization required when building? $ docker login [repository] -u [user] -p '[pass]'

for i in "${arr[@]}"
do

A disk cache is a software mechanism that allows the system to keep in RAM some data that is normally stored on a disk, so that further accesses to that data can be satisfied quickly without accessing the disk. There are at least three different types...

- Dentry cache, which is used by the VFS to speed up the translation of a file pathname to the corresponding inode

- Buffer cache, consisting of buffers (storing a single disk block)

- Page cache, each page in the cache corresponds to several blocks of a regular file or a block device file

Test a website / web app performance, in terms of time of connections.

time_namelookup – time, in seconds, it took from the start until the name resolving was completed. 

time_connect – time, in seconds, it took from the start until the TCP connect to the remote host (or proxy) was completed. 

time_pretransfer – time, in seconds, it took from the start until the file transfer was just about to begin. 

time_starttransfer – time, in seconds, it took from the start until the first byte was just about to be transferred. 

time_total – total time, in seconds, that the full operation lasted (millisecond resolution).
Get fingerprints of all authorized_keys for a certain user.

# in his/her .bashrc
function fingerprints() {
  local file="${1:-$HOME/.ssh/authorized_keys}"
  while read l; do
    [[ -n $l && ${l###} = $l ]] && ssh-keygen -l -f /dev/stdin <<<$l
  done < "${file}"
}
$ source ~/.bashrc
$ fingerprints

# /etc/ssh/login-notify.sh
#!/bin/sh
# Change these two lines:
sender="sender-address@example.com"
recepient="notify-address@example.org"

if [ "$PAM_TYPE" != "close_session" ]; then
    host="`hostname`"
    subject="SSH Login: $PAM_USER from $PAM_RHOST on $host"
    # Message to send, e.g. the current environment variables.

    echo "$message" | mailx -r "$sender" -s "$subject" "$recepient"
fi

Let's do some Logrotate 

- Configure it for a specific file

- Force the rotation now

- Check which files are being rotated, and the date of the last rotation

# /etc/logrotate.d/php_errors.conf
# comments must be on their own separate lines
/var/log/php_errors.log {
   # how often
   daily
   # how many files to keep
   rotate 10 
    # if this file is missing, just go to another one and don't throw an error
   missingok
   # don't rotate if it's empty... if you want to force it anyway, just use "ifempty"
   notifempty 
   # compression
   compress 
   # max-size before rotating
   maxsize 20M  
   # add date extension
   dateext 
   # date format
   dateformat .%Y.%m.%d 
   # where to move the rotated log files
   olddir [old_dir_name] 
   # permissions of the new log file to be created
   create [mode] [owner] [group] 
   # user for rotation
   su [owner] [group]
}

# just want a simulation? use -d
$ logrotate -fvd /etc/logrotate.d/php_errors.conf

$ cat /var/lib/logrotate/status
logrotate uses crontab to work. It's scheduled work, not a daemon, so no need to reload its configuration.

The Linux bonding driver provides a method for aggregating multiple network interface controllers into a single logical bonded interface of two or more NIC slaves.

The majority of modern Linux distributions come with a Linux kernel which has the Linux bonding driver integrated as a loadable kernel module and the ifenslave user-level control program pre-installed. But... more generally, what is bonding?

Also called:
  • Link Aggregation
  • Ethernet bonding / teaming
  • NIC bonding / teaming
  • Port aggregation / channeling / trunking
Link aggregation allows you to combine multiple Ethernet links into a single logical link between two networked devices. Link aggregation offers the following benefits:
  • Increased reliability and availability. If one of the physical links in the LAG goes down, traffic is dynamically and transparently reassigned to one of the other physical links.
  • Better use of physical resources. Traffic can be load-balanced across the physical links.
  • Increased bandwidth. The aggregated physical links deliver higher bandwidth than each individual link.
  • Cost effectiveness. A physical network upgrade can be expensive, especially if it requires new cable runs. Link aggregation increases bandwidth without requiring new equipment.
 The most common device combinations involve connecting a switch to another switch, a server, a network attached storage (NAS) device, or a multi-port access point.

# simple way
$ hostname -I

Network Address Translation (NAT), typically necessary in LANs with private IP addresses, is implemented trasparently by stateful firewalls. We have:

- Source NAT (SNAT) - altering source IP (first package opening a connection), during post-routing
Ex: masquerading, all source IPS of a LAN are converted to a (dinamically assigned) sigle source IP. 

- Destination NAT (DNAT) - altering destination IP (first package opening a connection), during pre-routing. 
Ex: redirect web traffic to a local server, or maybe redirect all connections to a transparent proxy.

# DNAT - Change destination IP to 192.168.1.1:8080
$ iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING  -p tcp -d 157.138.7.88 --dport 80 -j DNAT –to-destination 192.168.1.11[-192.168.1.13]:8080

# DNAT - Redirection to transparent proxy 
$ iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -j REDIRECT -p tcp --dport 80 --to-port 3128

# SNAT - Masquerading, for dynamically-assigned IPs
$ iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ens33 -j MASQUERADE

# SNAT - Change source IP to 157.138.22.6:8080
$ iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ens33 -j SNAT --to 157.138.22.6:8080

iptables rules with CIDR notation — two fundamental cases:

- Write an iptables rule to DROP incoming FTP (port 21) traffic from the entire source network 157.138.22.0

- Write an iptables rule to ACCEPT forwarded FTP (port 21) connections to host 192.168.1.110 from source network 192.168.100.0

$ iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 21 -s 157.138.22.0/24 -j DROP

$ iptables -A FORWARD -p tcp --dport 21 -d 192.168.1.110 -s 192.168.100.0/24  -j ACCEPT
As usual, /24 can equivalently be written as /255.255.255.0
Suppose a (remote or local) SSL/TLS certificate, in any port. How to check it and how to download it?

#  check expiration date only? add a pipe: | openssl x509 -noout -dates 
$ openssl s_client -showcerts -connect google.com:443 -status </dev/null

# download (PEM format)
$ openssl s_client -connect www.google.com:443 2>/dev/null </dev/null | sed -ne '/-BEGIN CERTIFICATE-/,/-END CERTIFICATE-/p'

The fact that it's working for you does not it's working for everbody (or viceversa)! Look at that Certificate Chain. Wanna find which one could be causing problems for other people? After giving the command above, copy the various BEGIN...END certificates in different files, and for each one use $ openssl x509 -noout -dates < /tmp/some_file

A root certificate is a public key self-signed certificate that identifies a root certificate authority (CA). This means the “Issuer” and ”Subject” are the same. A root certificate becomes a trusted root certificate (or trusted CA, or trust anchor) by virtue of being included by default in the trust store of a piece of software such as a browser or OS.

How can I check the installed ones?
$ awk -v cmd='openssl x509 -noout -subject' ' /BEGIN/{close(cmd)};{print | cmd}' < /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
They are updated with OS releases, but sometimes you might need to update 'em manually. Check instructions for your distro.
Let's talk semidivine amity. What's the difference between postfix/smtp and postfix/smtpd? And what about main.cf and master.cf?

postfix/smtpd - This is typically the SMTP daemon process for handling incoming mail and routing to the appropriate internal location.
postfix/smtp - This is typically the SMTP daemon process for delivering mail out to the world.

main.cf sets the default values used by all services defined in master.cf; options (with the -o flag) in master.cf can override these on a per-service basis.
What is UUCP?

UUCP is an acronym of Unix-to-Unix Copy. The term generally refers to a suite of computer programs and protocols allowing remote execution of commands and transfer of files, email and netnews between computers.

Despite a serious lack of sex-appeal, email via UUCP over TCP is a practical option for sites without permanent Internet connections, and for sites without a fixed IP address.

a filesystem service which implements a union mount for other file systems: allows files and directories of separate file systems (branches) to be transparently overlaid, forming a single coherent file system. From an executable (binary file), produce:

- A text file with hex codes (machine language), modify it and regenerate the binary

- A text file with Intel 64 (assembly language). This is what we call disassemble or decompile

$ xxd -g 1 count > count.txt # -g 1 is to write distinct bytes
$ xxd -r count.txt > count2 # this is to be executed after modifying the file!
$ chmod +x count2

# disassemble (-d) displaying sections (-s), in Intel syntax (-M intel)
# "count" could even be an .exe file
$ objdump -ds -M intel [count] > [count.txt]



- "can be relocated" means that at run-time they might change! Important. 

- "as bytes" means that each part is a byte (55 = 1 part; 48 89 e5 = 3 bytes etc...). The first one is called the opcode, the other are parameters
Of course there are specific editors for binary files, like hexedit or even Sublime, but we want to be able to work from any kind of file.
How can i produce a checksum for file integrity and verify it?

# for a simple text, we would use "echo -n | sha512sum", where -n is to avoid outputting the trailing newline
$ sha512sum [some_dir]/* > checksum

# assuming we have [some_dir] in our current location
$ sha512sum -c checksum

Let's say we don't want to risk giving SUID root to a program (not even using privilege drop), for example ping. But still, we want Alice to be able to ping us (maybe prod us too).

Well, enter capabilities.

$ setcap cap_net_raw+ep /bin/ping

$ getcap /bin/ping
Adds the cap_net_raw capability to ping, which can now be run by user alice without the necessity of SUID root permission. Of course, capabilities are very spefic. Google is your friend.

You imported some files from a ISS to a Linux machine, and now...

- You realize they are fucked up filenames (e.g. accented letters) which won't be reached by a web browser!

- Moreover, you have files with shit like "^M" at the end of every line. These are the line endings: "$" is Unix \n or LF, while "^M$" is Windows \r\n or CRLF. These can be visualized using $ cat -e [file]

# recursive and will only keep filenames matching the "from" parameter!
# this is just a test... To actually convert, use --notest
# for some fucked up reason, just installing could fuck up Grub configuration... Think carefully before installing, and look for alternatives in the future!!!
# arabic is windows-1256
$ convmv -r -f windows-1252 -t UTF-8 *

# safer alternative?
# renames files by removing spaces + translate / cleanup Latin-1 (ISO 8859-1) characters encoded in 8-bit ASCII, Unicode characters encoded in UTF-8, and CGI escaped characters
$ detox -v *

# if it doesn't work, try using different mode: -c mac, -c iso, -c ascii, -c 7bit
$ dos2unix [filename]

Some basics of video editing

# Convert a MOV to a MP4
# the lower the qscale value, the bet­ter the qual­ity. Range from 1 (high­est qual­ity) to 31 (low­est qual­ity)
$ ffmpeg -i [input.mov] -q:v 0 [output.mp4]

# Remove audio from a video
$ ffmpeg -i [input] -c copy -an [output]

# Cut a fragment
# It's minutes, seconds and milliseconds, so the first 30 seconds it's just 00:00 and 00:30
# up to the end? just omit the -t parameter
$ ffmpeg -ss 00:00:00 -t 01:00:00 -i [input] -vcodec copy -acodec copy [output]

# Get specific info like CRF (Constant Rate Factor) or Bit Rate (8 bit or 10 bit)
# No, ffmpeg is not enough... Look for crf= in the "Encoding settings"
$ mediainfo [input]

# Optimize, that is reduce the CRF
# lower CRF --> higher bitrate/size/quality and viceversa. Experiment and see!
# x265 default CRF is 28, corresponding to libx264's default CRF of 23, but results in about half the file size
# but... Very little support! See https://caniuse.com/hevc
# Yes, the codec must be specified
$ ffmpeg -i [input.mp4] -vcodec libx265 -crf 28 [output]
$ ffmpeg -i [input.mp4] -vcodec libx264 -crf 23 [output]

For a standard HTML5 video, we should at least provide:

- Advanced Video Coding (AVC/H.264), in a mp4 container. Yes, there's a High Efficiency Video Coding (HEVC/H.265)... Alas, as of 2020, not widely supported (https://caniuse.com/hevc)! 

- VP9 by Google, in a webm container. Successor to VP8 and de facto also to the Xiph.org Foundation's Theora Video

How can we produce such files?

# the source file could also be libx265 (mp4 container)!
$ ffmpeg -i [input] -c:v libx264 -crf 30 -vf format=yuv420p -c:a copy [output.mp4]

$ ffmpeg -i [input] -c:v libvpx-vp9 -crf 30 -b:v 0 [output.webm]

Of course you can use different CRF values (0 is loseless; 18 virtually loseless etc) 

There's much more to know! See https://www.freecodecamp.org/news/video-formats-for-the-web/

How do I find out if my X.509 certificate is in PEM, DER, or PFX format? More generally, how can I read a certificate and see which domains is it valid for, or the signing authority etc?

# DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules)
# Encoded Certificate
# Binary 
$ openssl x509 -in cert.crt -inform DER -text

# PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail)
# Base64-encoded DER certificate
# Simple text
$ openssl x509 -in cert.crt -text

# P7C alias PKCS#7
# SignedData structure without data, just certificate(s) or Certificate Revocation List(s) (CRL)
# Binary
$ openssl pkcs7 -in cert.crt -info

# PFX alias P12 alias PKCS#12
# Certificate(s) (public) and private keys (password protected)
# Binary
$ openssl pkcs12 -in cert.crt -info
Try these commands and see which one can read the file!

Suppose that when you use file -i [certificate.crt | private.key] you get "data" and when priting that you get gibberish.

They're probably binary-based DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) files and now you need 'em in text-based PEM or P12 format...

# PEM
$ openssl x509 -in [der.crt] -inform der -out [cert.pem] -outform pem
$ openssl rsa -in [private.key] -inform der -out [priv.key.pem] -outform pem

# P12 (first you need the PEM)
$ openssl pkcs12 -export -inkey [cert.key] -in [cert.pem] -out [cert.p12]

Trim silence from audio files.

# start only -
$ sox [in.mp3] [out.mp3] silence 1 0.1 1%

# start + end 
$ sox [in.mp3] [out.mp3] silence 1 0.1 1% reverse silence 1 0.1 1% reverse

- SoX is, by their own definition, the Swiss Army knife of audio manipulation. For this problem, better than ffmpeg because it won't require re-encoding.

- The first 1 is the "above-period": means to only trim silence at the beginning (of the original direction, or after reversing)

- Trim silence (anything less than 1% volume) until we encounter sound lasting more than 0.1 seconds 

- Input and output file cannnot coincide!!! 

Gold: https://digitalcardboard.com/blog/2009/08/25/the-sox-of-silence/

#!/bin/sh

set -e

if [ "$2" = "suspend" ] || [ "$2" = "hybrid-sleep" ]

syndaemon monitors keyboard activity and disables the touchpad when the keyboard is being used.

-i 3 will wait 3 seconds after the last key press before enabling the touchpad (default is 2.0s).
-d starts syndaemon at the background.
-t disables tapping and scrolling, not mouse movements, in response to keyboard activity.
-k ignores modifier keys when monitoring keyboard activity.

Convert an mp4 to a GIF.

$ mkdir frames
$ ffmpeg -i video.mp4 -r 5 'frames/frame-%03d.png'
$ convert -layers Optimize -delay 20 -loop 0 frames/frame-*.png [myimage.gif]

-r 5 stands for FPS value - for better quality choose bigger number; but also adjust the value with the -delay in 2nd step following the equivalence table below.

-delay 20 means the time between each frame is 0.2 second. Choose according to this:

1 = 100 fps
2 = 50 fps
4 = 25 fps
5 = 20 fps
10 = 10 fps
20 = 5 fps 
25 = 4 fps 
50 = 2 fps 
100 = 1 fps

-loop 0 means repeat forever

-layers Optimize is the lazy man's way to optimize images. Too lossy? Try to remove it.

A SYN flood is of denial-of-service attack in which an attacker rapidly initiates a connection to a server without finalizing the connection. The server has to spend resources waiting for half-opened connections, which can consume enough resources to make the system unresponsive to legitimate traffic. The packet that the attacker sends is the SYN packet, a part of TCP's three-way handshake used to establish a connection.

How can we prevent it?

# Check - Many connections in that state? Then it could be happening...
$ netstat -tuna | grep :80 | grep SYN_RECV

# /etc/sysctl.conf
net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1
net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog = 2048
net.ipv4.tcp_synack_retries = 3
net.ipv4.netfilter.ip_conntrack_tcp_timeout_syn_recv=45
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1

$ sysctl -p

Given a netblock (200.200.0.0/16), we want to:

1. Determine live hosts, that is aLive IPs (Host Discovery)
A sixteen-bit long netmask means the network could contain up to 2^16 (65536) hosts with IP addresses in the 200.200.0.0 - 200.200.255.255 range. 
ICMP Ping sweeping tools perform the a PING to every host in a subnet or IP range, saving you from typing hundreds or thousands ping of commands: see below.

2. Determine if they have an associated host name/domain
What about it?

# -a is to show alive systems
# -g is to provide a list of IPs - we can use both CIDR notation of start/end range
# with the redirect, we just keep the valid addresses
$ fping -a -g 200.200.0.0/16 2>/dev/null
$ fping -a -g 200.200.0.0 200.200.255.255 2>/dev/null

# if you run the scan from a machine within the same network
# Nmap runs an ARP scan instead of sending ICMP packets
# To avoid this behavior, you can use the --disable-arp-ping or --send-ip option.
$ nmap -sn 200.200.0.0/16

# Find out DNS servers (Port TCP/UDP 53)
# Then we can perform a reverse lookup to find out if they are serving any particular domain, try a transfer zone etc
$ nmap -sS -p53 [NETBLOCK] 
$ nmap -sU -p53 [NETBLOCK]

Let's do some some banner grabbing. After service probing (see related card), it's time for:

- Active OS Fingerprinting (send packets and wait for response --> risk of detection!). See below.

- Passive OS Fingerpriting (analyze captured traffic, without sending packets). What about it?

# Most simple case! Look for "Server" and "X-Powered-by"
$ curl -I [domain]

# -Pn is to skip host discovery (treat all targets as online)
# in case it fails, the tool could suggest a SYN scan on the UDP ports with $ sudo nmap -sSU [host]
# ... but you could also go hardcore with $ sudo nmap -A -n [host]
$ sudo nmap -Pn -O [host]

# a basis signature file is included with the download package
# possibly, the hosts should be IPs, not hostnames
# -P0 is to avoid PING, which are normally swatted by servers
$ httprint -P0 -h [host_served_by_web-server] -s [signature file]

# Passive -p0f
# https://lcamtuf.coredump.cx/p0f3/

In the "active" domain, there are some ol' school methods we should know, too: the HEAD request has to be digited after opening a connection. BTW, Remember that it's HTTP/1.0 but HTTP/2 (without the .0!)

# NetCat - HTTP only
$ nc [host] [port]
HEAD / HTTP/1.0 

# OpenSSL - HTTPS too
$ openssl s_client -connect [target]:443
HEAD / HTTP/1.0

Here we have diagrams of a TCP (or UDP) Connect Scan, for a single open port

TCP Open
- Threeway Handshake 
- RST + ACK from the client to close connection

TCP Close
- SYN
- RST+ACK from the server to close connection

Useful if dealing with IPv6. But... Relies on the target OS (no raw packets). Less efficient! Moreover, every TCP connect scan probe gets recorded in the daemon logs because, from the application point of view, the probe looks like a legitimate connection! What about it?

# TCP
$ nmap -sT [target]

# UDP
$ nmap -sU [target]
$ nmap -sS [target]
TCP SYN scans (half-opening scans) were invented to be stealthy by design. During a SYN scan, the scanner does not perform a full handshake it just sends a SYN packet and analyzes the response.
We have seen TCP Connect Scans and TCP SYN (Stealth) Scans. One more to go... Version Detection Scan alias Service Probing.
$ nmap -sV <target>
Not stealth... But shall give information about the running daemons.

Let's analyze the output of this nbtstat command, to find out Windows share (from a Windows machine). Then, let's do the same for Linux.

 

- The record type <00> tells us that ELS-WINXP is a workstation. The type " UNIQUE " means the computer must have only one IP assigned. The second line contains the workgroup or the domain the computer is joined to.
- The record type <20> tell us that the server service (file sharing or printer sharing) is up and running on the machine; this means we can try to get some more information about it with NET VIEW.

# Lists local name table (if you are already on the local network!)
> nbtstat -n

# List remote machine name table
> nbstat -A [remote_IP]

# To be used on a Server Service (<20>) to find shares
> net view -A [remote_IP]

# K is the local share, C the remote one (found with the previous command)
> net use K: \\192.168.99.162\C

# maybe we should be using on the whole network [remote_net_IP]/24
$ nbtscan-v [remote_IP]
$ nmblookup -A [remote_IP]

# or, better... 
$ smbclient -L [remote_IP]
$ sudo mount.cifs //192.168.99.162/C /media/K_share/ user=,pass=
Using smbclient, we also see the administrative shares hidden by the Windows tools, that is those with "$" at the end.
One of the techniques to prevent a buffer overflow is Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR). What about it?

The application calculator (calc.exe) is opened in the debugger and has the base address of 00170000. After rebooting the machine and reloading calculator gain, we can see that the base address has changed to: 01040000

It's an OS module that introduces randomness for executables, libraries, and stacks in the memory address space; this makes it more difficult for an attacker
to predict memory addresses and causes exploits to fail and crash the process. When ASLR is activated, the OS loads the same executable at

https://github.com/pixelb/ps_mem

First Question: time

In (h)top, %CPU is the task's share of the elapsed CPU time since the last screen update, expressed as a percentage of total CPU time.

In ps, CPU usage is currently expressed as the percentage of time spent running during the entire lifetime of a process. Let's say you have a process that was started a week ago and during that time it used 2.2% of CPU time on average. If suddenly it would became CPU intensive ( constantly consuming 100%) - looking at ps you would observe same 2.2% for the first few hours.

Second Question: cores

In ps, 106% means it's occupying a full core plus a little of another. So, if you have say, 4 cores, a process that is multi-threaded (so it can handle pushing load to all cores) could reach 400%. 

Also top shows CPU usage as a percentage of a single CPU by default. You can change this behavior by pressing I (that's Shift + i and toggles "Irix mode") while top is running. That will cause it to show the pecentage of available CPU power being used.

CSS Sprites are a means of combining multiple images into a single image file for use on a website, to help with performance. 
While the total image size (sometimes) goes up with sprites, several images are loaded with a single HTTP request.
How to generate and how to use 'em?

# ImageMagick is always the shit
# This is what we generally mean for "combining images"
$ convert image1.png image2.png image3.png -append result/vertical-sprite.png
$ convert image1.png image2.png image3.png +append result/horizontal-sprite.png

# My Script
output_filename=$1
last_one="/home/$(whoami)/Pictures/$(ls -t /home/"$(whoami)"/Pictures | head -n 1)"
last_two="/home/$(whoami)/Pictures/$(ls -t /home/"$(whoami)"/Pictures | head -n2 | sort | head -n1)"
convert "$last_one" "$last_two" -append "$output_filename.png"

.flags-canada, .flags-mexico, .flags-usa { background-image: url('../images/flags.png'); background-repeat: no-repeat; } 
.flags-canada { height: 128px; background-position: -5px -5px; } 
.flags-usa { height: 135px; background-position: -5px -143px; } 
.flags-mexico { height: 147px; background-position: -5px -288px; }

You set the same background-image on several CSS classes and set the background position and dimensions of the individual classes to display a single portion of the sprite.  
For more on generation, see: https://css-tricks.com/css-sprites/

Optimize (reduce size) of  images:

- PNG

- JPG

# Lossless, but execution time may vary! Min compression is -o2, max is -o7
# Keep file system permission and make a backup of original PNG 
$ find . -type f -iname "*.png" -print0 | xargs -I {} -0 optipng -o5 -quiet -keep -preserve -log optipng.log "{}"

# Defaults are usually fine, but won't reduce that much. Use --size=xxk or --size=xxM if you feel like experimenting 
$ find . -type f -iname "*.jpg" -print0 | xargs -I {} -0 jpegoptim "{}"

A classical use case for a network namespace is to run an OpenVPN tunnel for specific applications.

# Create an --up and --down script for OpenVPN. This script will create the VPN tunnel interface inside a network namespace called vpn
# netsn-script.sh
#!/bin/sh
case $script_type in
        up)
                ip netns add vpn
                ip netns exec vpn ip link set dev lo up
                ip link set dev "$1" up netns vpn mtu "$2"
                ip netns exec vpn ip addr add dev "$1" \
                        "$4/${ifconfig_netmask:-30}" \
                        ${ifconfig_broadcast:+broadcast "$ifconfig_broadcast"}
                if [ -n "$ifconfig_ipv6_local" ]; then
                        ip netns exec vpn ip addr add dev "$1" \
                                "$ifconfig_ipv6_local"/112
                fi
                ;;
        route-up)
                ip netns exec vpn ip route add default via "$route_vpn_gateway"
                if [ -n "$ifconfig_ipv6_remote" ]; then
                        ip netns exec vpn ip route add default via \
                                "$ifconfig_ipv6_remote"
                fi
                ;;
        down)
                ip netns delete vpn
                ;;
esac

$ chmod +x netsn-script.sh
# Start OpenVPN and tell it to use our --up and --down script instead of executing ifconfig and route
$ openvpn --ifconfig-noexec --route-noexec --up netns-script.sh --route-up netns-script.sh --down netns-script.sh

# Now you can start programs to be tunneled
$ ip netns exec vpn [command]

For packet sniffing we could also use the good ol' dsniff, but it ain't supported anymore. So, let's found out some password credentials with tcpdump

# -vvv --> verbose
# -A --> print each packet in ASCII. Handy for capturing web pages
# -S --> print absolute, rather than relative, TCP sequence numbers
# -s --> snap length, see notes
# 0 --> MTU to 0 (entire package)
# dst --> destination

$ tcpdump -vvvASs 0 dst 192.168.102.139

Snap Length
Bytes of data from each packet rather than the default of 68 (adequate for IP, ICMP, TCP and UDP but may truncate protocol information from name server and NFS packets). Packets truncated because of a limited snapshot are indicated in the output with ‘‘[|proto]’’, where proto is the name of the protocol level at which the truncation has occurred. Note that taking larger snapshots both increases the amount of time it takes to process packets and, effectively, decreases the amount of packet buffering. This may cause packets to be lost. Setting snaplen to 0 means use the required length to catch whole packets.

dmesg says something like...

# Detected Hardware Unit Hang
# Reset adapter unexpectedly

# get card name according to Hetzner https://docs.hetzner.com/robot/dedicated-server/general-information/system-adjustments-after-server-replacement/
# works in rescue mode, too
$ udevadm test-builtin net_id /sys/class/net/eth0 | grep '^ID_NET_NAME_PATH'

The network interface is probably fucked up, or could be an issue with TCP Segmentation Offload (see related card).

- Ask your hosting provider to replace it, give you another one, or move the drives to another server
- Just get the name of the new physical interface (see code) and replace the original one in /etc/network/interfaces
- BUT... The values you get from $ udevadm test-builtin net_id /sys/class/net/eth0 are read from highest to lowest priority (see https://wiki.debian.org/NetworkInterfaceNames). In our case, we were trying to set in /etc/network/interfaces ID_NET_NAME_PATH (as per Hetzner documentation), but it actually needed ID_NET_NAME_ONBOARD. Understood thanks to syslog ("eno1: renamed from ...")

Magento Performance. What about the operating system level?

### /etc/sysctl.conf ###

# check actual number of established connections
$ netstat -an | grep ESTABLISHED | wc -l

# the Linux kernel supports a mechanism to “reuse” TCP connections
# more precisely, allows the reuse of a client port immediately after the connection has been closed, even though the connection is still in TIME_WAIT state
net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse = 1

# maximum number of open sockets waiting for connections
net.core.somaxconn = 1024

- Every KVM virtual machine has its own TCP/IP stack and this means these parameters can be applied to the single VM and not on the host.

- According to Cristiano Sensei, "il problema è comunque mitigato dal frontend che va in HTTP/2".

- This setting must *not* be applied when communication with hosts behind a NAT firewall needs to take place. Moreover, it must also *not* be applied if not all hosts that use a TCP connection to communicate with have TCP timestamps enabled. Otherwise you might encounter strange TCP connection issues after applying this configuration parameter (net.ipv4.tcp_timestamps = 1, which is the default on most modern systems).

Common crontabs tricks (and some errors in the notes)

# Start a GUI from crontab?
# find out your $DISPLAY envvar and then use it, always with the full path of the software

# run "every 10 minutes" 
*/10 * * * * /full/path/some_script.sh

# run "every 10 seconds"

# 1.30 AM, first day of the month, every three months, whatever day of the week
30 01 1 */3 * /my/other/script > /dev/null 2>&1

# 1.30 AM, first day of the month, month 3 and 5, whatever day of the week
30 01 1 3,5 * /my/other/script > /dev/null 2>&1

@reboot         # when the system boots / starts up / reboots
@yearly         # 00 00 1 1 *
@monthly      # 00 00 1 * *
@weekly        # 00 00 * * 0
@daily           # 00 00 * * *
@hourly         # 00 * * * *

# we can have hour ranges, but must be separate before and after midnight:
* 22-23 * * * (and a separate entry: * 00-05 * * *) /myscript

Slow disk? Too many iops?

# Check streaming I/O
# time should be fractions of a second and speed in the MB/s range
$ dd if=/dev/zero of=/root/testfile bs=512 count=5000 oflag=direct

# Latency
# time should be some seconds and speed in the MB/s or (better) GB/s range
$ dd if=/dev/zero of=/root/testfile bs=1024M count=1 oflag=direct

Plus, when running $ top you should see a high wa percentage. It should be 0.0% almost all the time. Values consistently above 1% may indicate that your storage device is too slow to keep up with incoming requests. Remember to expand the view by pressing ‘1’ on your keyboard to view ‘wa’ time for each CPU core when in use. If some cores have a high ‘%wa’ time... You have your bottleneck

or - when running $ iotop -oPa multiple processes are running with 99.99% IO

Find duplicates files and directories.

# files
$ rdfind -dryrun true /home/user

# then, choose one of the following
$ rdfind -makehardlinks true /home/user
$ rdfind -deleteduplicates true /home/user

# directories
find $(readlink -f $1) -type d | while IFS= read -r line
do
    cd "$line"
    children=$(find ./ -type f | sort)
    base=$(basename "$line")
    sum=$(echo $children $base | md5sum)
    echo $sum $line
done | sort -n | uniq -D -w 32

Suppose that your web server has a configuration with multiple virtual hosts whose document roots are actually symlinks. Like this:

/var/www/lenny/code -> /var/www/common_codebase
/var/www/karl/code -> /var/www/common_codebase

The reason we might want to do that is that within /var/www/lenny and /var/www/karl (that is, one level up from the document root) we might place a different .env file for each host, to be read from our application. But there's a catch or two...

- If our application/framework makes use of the Unix FS syntax (..) to go upwards, we must use only a symlink-aware function! See note below.

- If our application/framework has a DotEnv class or the like, we must make sure that while relying on the .env files it does set system environment variables. Otherwise, one configuration will topple all the others! This happens by default with Symonfy, as you can check with a $ printenv

In the case of PHP, getcwd(), realpath(), dirname() etc won't work! We need: dirname($_SERVER['SCRIPT_FILENAME']) or dirname($_SERVER['pwd']). Works both for the web and for the CLI, in the latter case according to the directory from where the script was called.

You're compiling drivers for a WI-FI adapter and it fails with this message:

Skipping BTF generation for ....ko due to unavailability of vmlinux

What about it?

$ apt install dwarves
$ cp /sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux /usr/lib/modules/$(uname -r)/build/

- BTF (BPF Type Format) is the metadata format which encodes the debug info related to BPF program/map. 

- The Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF) is a technology used in certain computer operating systems for programs that need to, among other things, analyze network traffic.

- DWARF is a debugging file format used by many compilers and debuggers to support source level debugging.

- vmlinux is the uncompressed version of kernel image,  which can be used for debugging.

zram, formerly called compcache, is a Linux kernel module for creating a compressed (= on the fly compression) block device in RAM, that be used for swap or as general-purpose RAM disk.

How?

$ sudo modprobe zram

# set compresison algorithm and disk size
$ echo lz4 | sudo tee /sys/block/zram0/comp_algorithm
$ echo 4G | sudo tee /sys/block/zram0/disksize

$ sudo mkswap /dev/zram0
$ sudo swapon /dev/zram0

# check
$ sudo zramctl
You should then configure a way to make it work at every reboot (systemd service file etc)
I want kernel security upgrades without rebooting the machine. How?

# Option 1: kernelCare
# works with most system, but no free tier
# https://docs.tuxcare.com/live-patching-services/

# Option 2: kSplice Linux Extension
# only for Oracle Linux or certain kernels of certain Ubuntu distributions (= "Free Desktop Edition")
# https://ksplice.oracle.com/

# Option 3: Canonical Livepatch Updates
# only for Ubuntu and moreover "free tier" means "beta testers tier"!
# https://www.reddit.com/r/Ubuntu/comments/tk5sv4/livepatch_tier_beta_stable/
$ sudo apt install ubuntu-advantage-tools
$ sudo pro attach [ubuntu-one-token-to-be-created-online]
$ canonical-livepatch status
I want security upgrades to my installed packages without rebooting

### Debian-based: unattended-upgrades ###

# check if it's up and running (should by default)
$ sudo systemctl status unattended-upgrades

# only enable -security repos
$ sudo nano /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades

$ sudo unattended-upgrades --dry-run

### RedHat-based ###
$ sudo dnf install dnf-automatic
$ sudo systemctl enable --now dnf-automatic.timer

# we could also choose to just download the updates, or be notified about 'em
$ sudo nano /etc/dnf/automatic.conf
Import a self-signed HTTPS certificate to PhotonOS

# verify openSSL directory (in our case /etc/ssl)
$ openssl version -d

# in some cases you might need to download the intermediate (subordinate) CAs from Let's Encrypt: curl -o e5.pem https://letsencrypt.org/certs/2024/e5-cross.pem
$ cp [cert.pem] /etc/ssl/certs/
$ openssl rehash /etc/ssl/certs
$ cat /etc/ssl/certs/*.pem > /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt
Copy from a remote server to a Google Drive (shared) folder

# install latest or distribution version might not support Google Drive (happened)
$ sudo -v ; curl https://rclone.org/install.sh | sudo bash

# use default settings (just press Enter) for most parameter, BUT...
# pay attention when it asks if it's a shared drive or not (= somebody shared it with you)
# to authenticate to Google, you'll also need to install rclone in another machine (desktop) which does have a browser and copy-paste the code
$ rclone config

# --tpslimit 5 --drive-upload-cutoff 1000T are mandatory if you wanna copy a file greater than 1.5 GB (otherwise: errors 429)
$ rclone copy -vP --tpslimit 5 --drive-upload-cutoff 1000T anci_backup_file_system.zip gdrive:

There are two main line encodings:

- Windows: CR (Carriage Return \r) and LF (LineFeed \n) pair (CRLF)
- OSX, Linux: LF (LineFeed \n) only

Suppose I have one repository but people working with both OS. What now?

# single-machine settings 
# Linux: Git will convert CRLF to LF when committing, but it won't modify line endings on checkout (you might need some dos2unix)
# Windows: Git will convert LF to CRLF on checkout but convert back to LF when committing
# Short: commits will always be LF; but Windows will still use CRLF
$ git config --global core.autocrlf input
ps> git config --global core.autocrlf true

# repo-wide settings
# .gitattributes
# force LF for all text files
 # but Windows scripts should keep CRLF
* text eol=lf
*.bat text eol=crlf
*.cmd text eol=crlf
*.ps1 text eol=crlf
